# Email drafts and sent emails for piccianeri.com

## **Sat 28 Sept 2024 (to schedule for Monday)**

Hey \[name\],

I’ve just delivered a bespoke accessibility workshop for a large organisation, and this is what I found.

70% of accessibility issues really do come from design.

And I’m not just talking about the client’s design issues (although I could write a book just about those).

In the course of my research I consulted hundreds of resources on accessibility. 

Most of them had very good advice on a specific topic, but in the meantime their website had:

1. Super-long line length (120 characters in one case). So bad for legibility, and for the cognitive load. I get to half that line and I’m completely lost – my frazzled brain can’t cope.  
2. Centred text galore. Also very bad for legibility, for people with low vision or dyslexia.  
3. No alt text, or badly written alt text that does no justice to the image in the context.  
4. Teeny tiny text.   
 


18 Oct

Tell you the truth, {{contact.full\_name}}: I do. They're so good for the ecosystem, they get rid of annoying insects in the home, plus in Italy they're taken to be a good omen that money is on its way.

 

(Not to mention their essential role at Halloween – coming up soon\!). 

 

And finally: aren't spider webs covered in dew a thing of beauty? 

Yes. Yes they are.

 

Now do you ever stop and remember why the World Wide Web – this informally organised network of interlinked hypertext documents stored on web servers that communicate with browsers using http, and cross-linked with one another – is called "web"?

 

Well it's because of those cross-links. Imagine them all linked up – and there you go: a beautiful spider web. 

 

So here's your poetic image for the weekend: links are the silk threads that weave the web.

Without links, the web would not be a web\!

Links are like magic, dew-covered threads to guide your visitors to where you want them to go.

A big part of accessibility is making sure that all of them can actually follow the trail, without bad surprises.

 

(A bad surprise would be to be eaten by the spider, but that's where the analogy ends). 

 

So as a pre-weekend gift, here are 4 golden rules to make sure the silk weave of your weblinks is strong, and everyone can navigate it:

– First golden rule:  
Make the links understandable even when out of context. This helps both visitors and screen readers to scan the page and work out what is of interest to them.  
Yes, this means never using “Click here” or “Read more” ever again.  
In a grid with blog articles: make the article title a link. This way, you won’t need “read more”.

In the text: “Click here” to learn how to write descriptive links can become “Learn how to write descriptive links”.  
Clearer content \= better SEO \= better accessibility.

– Second golden rule:

No bad surprises.   
If you want the link to open in a new tab, add “Opens in a new window”.  
(don't know how? here's how: [https://bit.ly/40gIbSW)](https://bit.ly/40gIbSW\))  
If the link is to a downloadable document, mention it in the link text itself.  
In a nutshell: use the link text to clarify where it goes. No exceptions.

– Third golden rule: underline your links.  
Not everybody can tell the difference between your body text colour, and your link colour. So please underline your links: this way, your visitors will know that they are a link, with no risk of misunderstandings. And if you think it's ugly, there are techniques to make it much nicer: [https://rafaltomal.com/tips/link-underlines/](https://rafaltomal.com/tips/link-underlines/)

– Fourth golden rule: style hover and focus states.  
Hover will confirm that an underlined link is indeed an interactive element; and focus will allow keyboard users to easily navigate the links on your page. Remember: not everybody uses a mouse, and everybody uses the web their own way – possibly different from yours\!

Is this all there is to perfect links? No, but it’s a great start.

Make your threads easy to follow, for better SEO for your business – and a better web for all\! Once again, an accessible feature turns out to be of great advantage for all.

Want to learn hands-on about how to write and style links?

 

Keep your eyes peeled on my emails – I'm working on something new to teach that, and many other things.

Hey {name}, 

Want to know the secret to standing out online?

Get your colour and typography right.

Too simple? No, I really do mean it. And I also know that you’ve heard me say it before. This is why I keep repeating it:

In the year 2024, 81% of the top 1 million websites still get colour and contrast wrong. It is by far the most common mistake. This is public data. 

As for my personal experience, after hundreds of audits and dozens of in-company workshops, I also always encounter the same reality: a good 60%-70% of accessibility issues do stem from design.

And by “design” I often mean: colour, contrast, and typography. 

**There is no doubt in my mind that teaching people how to get their colour, contrast and typography right would be a giant first step towards making the web a better, more inclusive place.** 

Want to get your content seen? Easy: learn how to use colour, contrast and typography properly.

So that’s why today I am here to announce:

**Get it Seen**

A colour, contrast and typography masterclass

## **Email Mon 4 Nov**

You know how some people think their work is above criticism?

You also know that many of them are designers, don’t you.

There is even a famous designer who used to say:

“I don’t deliver options. I deliver solutions”

(perhaps Paul Rand? Help\! Can’t remember…)

I am a designer, but I have to admit: 

I don’t agree – and I don’t care.

Client likes the design? Great.

Client doesn’t like the design? Also great, when they have good reasons and constructive feedback. I’ve thanked many a client for improving my designs with their observations. My “solution” was made better by their input. 

But when it comes to how I feel about feedback of something I designed – I don’t feel much. And because you know I’m a potty-mouthed scoundrel, I could even say that I don’t actually give a flying F.

I don’t understand those designers who get on a high horse about their originations, defending them to the death with uncouth clients who dare having an opinion. 

Often, clients know their business better than we do, however much research we've done on it. So they are in a great position to express their point of view. 

And if they want changes made, let’s make them. 

It is not me. I am not my design. It doesn’t matter.

All that matters is:

– Does the design serve the people using it?

– Does the design serve the business paying for it?

This, is all that matters.

Anything else, is just a designer’s ego screaming into the void. 

So when I keep hammering on about the same accessible design topics, you can trust that it’s not to serve my ego: it’s because I know they will make a design serve its users better. 

If a website looks great to the designer, and even to the client, but it’s a minefield of accessibility mistakes, and many people won’t be able to use it : that’s the hill I will die on. 

And that is why I decided to hold another masterclass on colour, contrast, and typography, and I called it Get it Seen – because ultimately if a portion of your audience can’t see a design, what’s the point of the design? 

And so many designs I encounter when working with clients are exactly like that: so often invisible to so many people.

I want to change that with the Get it Seen masterclass.

I told you about it last week, and I’m reminding you today because the coupon TOMATO that gets you €20 off expires the day after tomorrow: I don’t want you to miss out on it.   
The workshop will be held live on Tue 26 Nov at 4pm CET, but if you can’t attend don’t worry: not only will you get the recording with all transcripts, you will also get a bonus Q\&A session a few weeks after – so you can ask those burning questions. 

Email Wed 6 Nov

I live very close to the scene of a natural disaster of catastrophic proportions. Last week the western suburbs of Valencia, Spain, were hit by a huge flood that swept away bridges, houses, cars, and people like twigs in the wind.

Organising the armies of volunteers who have flocked to succour their neighbours in need isn't easy. It's not even that easy to work out where to go and help: thankfully, a lot of websites have sprung up to provide vital information. 

Not so thankfully, the vast majority of them are virtually invisible: they blithely ignore

Hey {{contact.full\_name}},

 

Life goes on, or so we're told.

That's what must happen after a natural catastrophe, or a human-made one: we roll up our sleeves, and soldier on. It's the logical consequence. 

I've been volunteering locally after a huge flood swept away bridges, buildings, cars, and people like twigs in the wind very close to where I live (my home and family are unscathed. I am beyond lucky). 

Besides raw manual labour, I am also helping out with tech stuff, because guess what: so many of the emergency websites that have been set up to help, are in fact inaccessible.

And guess what the most common problems are?

Yep: colour, contrast, and typography.

Being able to see what's on a screen matters very much, when it means the difference between getting vital aid, or not. 

Invisible help is not helpful.

So this is just a quick email to remind you that you've got until 23:59 Central European Time, that is to say UTC+1, to use the "TOMATO" coupon for a €20 discount on [**Get it Seen**](https://piccianeri.com/get-it-seen/): a masterclass on colour, contrast, and typography. To get your content seen, once and for all.

The masterclass will be held live on [Tue 26 Nov at 4pm CET](https://everytimezone.com/s/1fc396d7), but if you can’t attend, don’t worry: not only will you get the recording with all transcripts, you will also get a bonus Q\&A session a couple of weeks after.

P.s. A percentage of the earnings will go to the Valencia flood victims. I’m volunteering with the Fundación Altius. I know that there are so many good causes to give to, but if you want to help directly, [this page has donation information](https://www.fundacionaltius.org/altius-ayuda-valencia/).

 

 

###  **Mon 9 Dec – Hatch is live**

Finally, after months of bragging to anyone who would care to listen that I had been given the blessing to call out dicks on stage repeatedly, I can prove that it really did happen\!

The talk “The European Accessibility Act is coming to bite you in the arse”, given by me at Hatch Conference in Berlin last September, is free for you to watch on YouTube.

I will never watch it again because all I can see are my wrinkles (or should I say, the book that life is writing across my face, stories that all mean the years I own). 

But now you can, and you can get your teams and bosses to watch it, too – it’s not very long, anyway – and it will give you much needed ammunition to persuade your bosses or clients that they need to get their arse in gear in time for the European Accessibility Act next year. There is still time\!

 In case you need extra help with your persuasion efforts, here's a reminder of a couple of docs I created, that may help you:  
And  in case you need even extra extra help, please do write back or book a call with me: we can discuss how I can help you make sure your organisation or clients avoid being bitten in the arse come June 2025\.

### **Fri 13 Dec – creativity & accessibility**

You know what the problem with accessibility truly is?

It’s that accessibility is considered really boring at best, and a total pain in the arse at worst.

Boring (especially for designers, or design-adjacent peeps):  
– The old chestnut that “it stunts creativity”: can’t have stuff flying around the screen willy-nilly no more, or use “bold” colour combos that might hurt someone’s eyes, or incomprehensible typography

Pain in the arse:   
– have to spend money on remediating, rebuilding and training, in constant fear of legal retaliation.

Now, I won’t lie to you. There have been moments when having to make something accessible has been – let’s say, complicated. Mostly, the “complication” was created by the client’s reluctance to accept that a certain accessibility feature was essential. 

But I’d be lying if I didn’t confess that I’ve had to adapt the way I think about design.

I am, after all, a recovering creative – and I can assure you that past me (the one designing film posters, for instance) didn’t give visual accessibility a second thought – ever. First item on the list was: get bums on cinema seats; second was: do it in the coolest, most visually appealing, most creative, cleverest way possible. 

But I tell you what the essential ingredient to get to the best solution always was:

A good brief. 

You know the old adage “garbage in, garbage out”, I’m sure. Any successful design comes from a good brief. Plus, the hardest thing of all is to create without a strict brief: “free rein”, i.e. a lack of indications, often leads to despair. Too many options \= no options.

So that’s how I’d love for you, my designer or design-adjacent peeps, to consider accessibility:

As an excellent brief. 

Can you be accessible, and creative?

Yes. Yes you can. 

In the new year I will embark on a big educational project for a global organisation, and that’s exactly the angle I will work from, to make sure I get the design teams on board out of their own conviction – not just because legal said they must.

Can you think of a way you can use accessibility as a vehicle for creativity? I can – and will tell you, too. 

Have a wonderful weekend 

Tuesday 17 Dec 2024 – what3words

How did I end up in Maria Elena, a tiny village in Antofagasta, Chile?

Simple: thanks to action, persistence, and abundance.

I’m in a great membership programme, and our beloved leader got us to do an end-of-year exercise to close 2024, and move into 2025\.

In the final part of the exercise, we had to come up with 3 power words to take us through the new year. I toyed with many combinations: it is surprisingly difficult to pick 3 words, and 3 only.

Finally, I settled on:

Action – I need to be more proactive instead of reactive, and to make sure I don’t drop the ball whenever I get absorbed by work. 

Persistence – I know that that’s the key. It just is. It’s the key to almost everything in life – from mastering a skill, to conquering the world. I am good at it – that’s how I’m a decent yogi, skater, designer, painter, friend – but only intermittently. And intermittent persistence is not a thing. 

Abundance – it will be the inevitable, effortless consequence of action and persistence. Doesn’t it feel great\!

So far, so good – but how did I end up in Chile, then?

Well. After I finished the exercise, I hired a car and got driving towards a village down the Spanish coast from me. I kept thinking about my words for 2025: Action, Persistence, Abundance. Then, suddenly, a bank of dense fog enveloped the car – and the world around me became completely white. 

I slowed down considerably and kept driving through thick cotton wool, until the bank finally lifted. The scenery around me had changed: no sea on my left anymore, no green mountains on my right… just a desert, and a small village with a white church.

It was Maria Elena, in the region of Antofagasta, Chile. 

Or was it?

No. No it wasn’t.

I have been dreaming all my life of driving through the fog and finding myself in another continent, but it hasn’t happened yet. Sorry. Much to my disappointment, and at this point yours, too, I was still on my way to Denia, in the Comunidad Valenciana.

What did happen though, is that I went to the website what3words.com, put in my 3 power words for 2025:

Action.persistence.abundance

And the map placed me in Maria Elena, in the region of Antofagasta, Chile.

So cool\!

What 3 words is a fab thing I had never heard of\! Have you??

The folks at What 3 words divided the world into 3 metre squares and gave each square a unique combination of three words. This has a myriad of applications when you need to pin point a location that has no address. 

Or when you pick your 3 power words and want to see where you end up\!

So here’s my end-of-year suggestion for you.

– Pick your 3 power words

– Place them in what3words dot com

– See where that gets you

– Buy a ticket and go there in 2025\!

Hard to do when you end up in the middle of the Pacific Ocean perhaps, but you could fly over it. 

Where did your 3 words get you to? Do share, and let’s dream of travelling the world in 2025\!

Image description: the church in the village of María Elena, a white building with grey architectural details, a gabled roof, and an octagonal, short and squat bell tower above the portal. Nobody in the picture. The sky above is blue, with sparse white clouds. 

### **Thu 2 Jan 2025 – draft – didn’t send**

New Year’s resolutions are made to be broken. 

If you live in the northern hemisphere, January is often the worst month of the year: cold, darkness, and tax bills to pay. 

In and of itself, the whole “new year” concept is meaningless. There is no difference at all between 31 Dec 2024 and 1 Jan 2025\. Nothing has changed on a molecular level: it is all a construct, one of the many us humans create to feel better about our condition. 

However, there is a long gap between mid-December and the beginning of January when the Western world simply stops. I often find it annoying – especially when finance departments go on holiday before paying all freelancer’s invoices – but I also take it as an opportunity to reflect and plan.

Here’s what I want out of the new year as a designer:

Action

Do I ever follow my plans?

Hmmm… sort of.

But then again, someone great once said “Life is what happens to you while you're busy making other plans”.

(Surviving January if you live in the UK, for instance, is an achievement already: don’t make your life even more difficult by adding nicotine withdrawal, or a punishing fitness plan. (I stopped smoking in December 17 years ago precisely for this reason, and it worked).)

dishonesty is not a good business strategy.

Overlays are NOT the solution if you want an accessible website \- now everyone will know they’re not honest, either.


### **Fri 10 Jan – using the yellow colour post from 2018**

If there is one thing I miss about living in Italy, it’s the clothes markets. A riot of colours and unbelievable bargains, and nope, not shoddy quality, either.

So when I was in Siena over Christmas, I went to the market and I bought myself a bright yellow jumper (for the princely sum of 8 euros).

Ah, the sheer joy of it\!

This jumper seems to have the magic power of making other people happy, too. Every single person I’ve met when wearing it has made a positive comment about it. 

It’s the perfect shade of yellow, with just enough red in it to remind you of sunflowers, but not enough to remind you of egg yolks. Looks great with black and I feel like a bee\! 

But what’s yellow to you? 

Close your eyes now. What’s the first thing that comes to your mind, when I scream at you: YELLOW\! 

Is it a pencil, by any chance?

I wouldn’t be surprised if it were. 

In 1893 the Czech art supplies brand Koh-I-Noor produced the first wood-encased graphite pencil, painted externally. 

And they decided to paint it in yellow. 

Clearly, it was the right choice: Koh-I-Noor sold millions of those first yellow pencils. So many, in fact, that the yellow pencil has become the pencil archetype.

Take the pencil emoticon, for instance: ✏️. Yep – yellow. Do a Google image search: I bet you get the same.

 In our collective imagination, a 'true' pencil has to be yellow. 

(Such is the power of a good marketing decision\!)

Yellow is a positive colour in many cultures. In Africa it's often reserved to high-ranking aristocrats; in Japan it represents bravery; in Thailand it's the colour of luck; in imperial China, a particular shade was reserved for emperors. 

 

But\! Yellow is not always all positive. In English, cowards have a 'yellow streak'; in Germany, jealousy is yellow; in medieval France, criminals' doors were painted yellow; in the 1800s, books with yellow covers were sensationalist at best, and scandalous at worst. A colder yellow with a tinge of green is the colour of jaundice and ill health. And then there’s Ryanair’s acid yellow, of course: the perfect shade of what I call “vile yellow”. 

Yet, blog posts on colour psychology will almost invariably tell you that yellow is the happiest colour on the spectrum, evoking sunshine, hope, laughter, warmth, and energy. 

What they often fail to point out, however, is that yellow is also one of the easiest colours to get wrong online.

Why? 

Here’s why:

– Yellow on a screen can bring eye strain and fatigue: our long and medium cones are highly sensitive to its wavelength.

– Yellow colours on a digital platform often have 80-100% saturation and brightness: this can almost physically hurt sensitive eyes.

– Yellow text on a white background is too low contrast to be legible; reversing background and foreground won’t help.

– Yellow is not easy to tweak for contrast: darkening it makes it brown which definitely doesn’t provide the same “happy yellow” effect now, does it. 

So, should you use yellow online, at all?

… it depends. 

Is your pencil yellow?

Idea: creativity as tied to technology – think punk photocopies etc. (from Dizzy Users talk too)

Hey {{contact.first\_name}},

 When I was about 8 or 9, I was obsessed with weaving beads bracelets on my wooden loom.

 

I was so proud of that loom. It was just a plank of rough wood with a row of nails at either end, but I'd built it myself, hammering down the nails on a wider plank than usual, which would allow me to weave taller, wider bracelets.

 

I loved designing the patterns in my sketchbook, carefully working out how to reproduce them on the loom, square by square. But my favourite thing, by far, was choosing the colour schemes.

 

I still vividly remember the sharp pang of pleasure I felt when I matched bright, electric blue and deep orange beads as the colour scheme for a geometrical pattern. I had no idea that I'd picked two complementary colours: a bold pair of contrasting opposites that made my spirit fizz and and my loom sparkle. 

 

And I also had no idea at the time that the way I saw, savoured, and experienced those two vibrant colours could be totally different for someone else.

 

When I was about 8 or 9, I was obsessed with weaving beads bracelets on my wooden loom:  a plank of rough wood with a row of nails at either end that I'd proudly built by myself, hammering down the nails with relish (it was the 70s – not so hot on health and safety). 

I loved designing the patterns in my sketchbook – but my favourite activity, by far, was choosing the colour schemes.

 

I still vividly remember the sharp pang of pleasure I felt when I matched electric blue and deep orange beads as the colour scheme for a geometrical pattern. I had no idea that I'd picked two complementary colours: a bold pair of contrasting opposites that made my spirit fizz and and my loom sparkle. 

 

Some time later, my older brother was getting ready to go to a family wedding, and asked for advice on choosing a matching tie (remember this was Italy, where sartorial matters have a higher weight than elsewhere).  
   
We saw the options he'd aligned on the bed and oh my. He'd chosen a bright red tie that clashed garishly with the green suit. Think parrot effect. When we questioned his choice, my brother replied:  
"That tie is brown. It goes perfectly well with the green suit".  
   
😳, went our faces.  
   
I ran to get my favourite bracelet. I showed him the dazzling blue and orange combination that gave a spring to my step any time I wore it. He replied:  
   
"That's green and grey".  
   
Reader, you've guessed it: my brother is colour blind. And that's the day I found out.  
 My excitement at the vibrant combo was totally lost on him.

 

But his colour truth is still perfectly valid\! There can be no right or wrong: he is entitled to his own colour truth. Bright orange to me, muddy green to him: who is to say which one is "right"?

 

So, does this make designing with colour a total nightmare?

 

Well, it might for some – but most definitely not for those who join my masterclass next week\! Yep, here I am, harping on about it again, because I'm working on it right now and I **know** it will be great.

A [colour, contrast and typography masterclass](https://piccianeri.com/get-it-seen/)

Live online, on [Thursday 25 February 2025](https://everytimezone.com/s/17093bec)

 

A 3-hour long roller coaster ride on all things colour, from opinions and biased views, to incontrovertible colour facts; with a chapter on typography and images, and how to make them all play nice together – most importantly, in an accessible and compliant way.

What we'll do on the day

Here's a list of some of the things we'll do together:

* We'll look at the role of colour in different cultures (hint: don't wear white at a Chinese wedding)  
* We'll nerd out on colour systems, spaces and models (do you know the difference, and why it matters?)  
* We'll play with new techniques that work much better with the human eye (OKLCH, anyone?)  
* We'll discover what alternatives to colour we can use, and when  
* We'll build accessible palettes using future-facing techniques (you really do need to find out about OKLCH\!)  
* We'll check our work using APCA (perfect companion for OKLCH, and the future of contrast compliance)  
* We'll make sense of WCAG success criteria, without suffering  
* Cherry on the cake: of course we'll talk about typography, too\!

Why you should do it

1\. Because you will have fun

2\. And most definitely because you will acquire new skills and knowledge that will expand your market appeal to both clients and employers.

 

Accessible design is the way forward my friends\! Don't get left behind.

 

You might remember the image above – I wrote to you a while ago about it, casting aspersions on the terrible choice the designers made when they decided to pair such a bright yellow with white.

 

If you put that white and yellow combo – \#FFE000 as on Tony's website – in a [contrast checking app](https://colorcontrast.app/#fg=%23ffe000&bg=%23fff&level=aa&format=rgb&algo=WCAG2&filter=none), it is immediately obvious that whichever guidelines you use, whether APCA or WCGA, this "bold design choice" is irredeemably unusable. The contrast is too low, which means that too many people would not be able to read it.

 

But there's more. When I visit [the product page on the Tony's website](https://uk.tonyschocolonely.com/products/milk-almond-honey-nougat-32-180g), my eyes physically hurt.

 

The \#FFE000 yellow colour – ["School bus yellow" is its name](https://chir.ag/projects/name-that-color/#FFE000) – has 100% saturation, and 100% brightness. This by itself is already a problem, when looking at a backlit computer screen: way too much light hurting my eyes.

 

And on top of that: it's \*yellow\*.

But only if you [enroll now](https://piccianeri.com/enroll-atm-2024/)\!  Mon 3 March – Pink for boys  Pink is not a colour on the visible light spectrum: there is no pink wavelength. Look at the rainbow: no pink in there\! But it doesn't mean that pink doesn't exist. Pink is a reflective colour, not a transmissive one: it's created by pigments, and light only bounces off it – so it's not present in light, like the colours in the spectrum. |
| :---- |

## **Mon 24 March – data viz**

Hey {{contact.first\_name}},

 

Are you good with data and numbers? 

 

Some people are very good at all that – and I'm not one of them. While I don't think I have dyscalculia, I'm definitely mathematically challenged, and  have been since as long as I can remember.

 

Precisely for this reason I'm in a good position to work with data visualisations: if I can understand it, then anyone can\!

 

Data visualisations need to tell a story: and quite often, they fail.

 

Take the bar chart below, for example.

It's a horizontal bar chart illustrating the annual household income percentages in a geographical area, going from less than $20k a year (16%) to more than $200k a year (27%). The data set is pretty simple: and yet, when I look at this chart, I struggle to work out which story it's trying to tell me.

The fact that all the numerical data is on the same line confuses me; and the fact that each bar is a percentage, but I have no idea of what the whole that each percentage is part of looks like, confuses me even more. I find it hard to form a mental picture of the income levels: which means this chart has failed.

 

That's when I thought: this bar chart should really be a pie chart.

## **Tue 1 April – acronyms**

From Perplexity (see Ideas tab for the original text)

You know one thing that native English-speaking people seem to be very fond of – but that can be a mystery to non-natives?

Acronyms.

Yes – they are ubiquitous in English, the language I speak and write 80% of the time. And yet, they are another one of my pet peeves (I know, I do have a few).

I remember when I first started making British friends: in general I always understood, but sometimes my new mates seemed to be using a cryptic language that was only for the initiated.

“What time are you leaving home? Please let me know your eeteea”

What on earth is an eeteea when it’s at home? I wondered. This was pre-internet, remember, because I am ancient, so I couldn’t ask Google. I asked my friend instead, and she revealed the mystery: ETA \= Estimated Time of Arrival.

I soon realised that if I wanted to speak English like a native, I’d have to learn all these mysterious ciphers, and their hidden meaning: ASAP, AWOL, FUBAR\*… or what some people really meant when they said sarcastically, "All right mate, ["See You Next Tuesday"](https://www.dictionary.com/e/slang/see-you-next-tuesday/).

Interestingly enough, so many words that we now take for granted as complete nouns in their own right, are actually acronyms: for instance, RADAR stands for Radio Detection And Ranging – did you know? While SONAR stands for Sound Navigation And Ranging. In theory, we should write them out in capitals, but these words have entered dictionaries a while ago, and their origin as acronyms is now mostly forgotten. But I digress\!

Post-internet the acronyms frenzy only got worse. There are SO many acronyms out in the wild now. They’re everywhere – no getting away from them. 

But here’s the thing: acronyms are not accessible.

Why? Because not everyone knows what they mean, and assuming they do creates barriers. I, for one, often get my knickers in a twist with acronyms: I don’t remember them, unless they are pervasively obvious; I can struggle to pronounce them, because I am non-native and spelling is an acquired skill for me; plus, because they’re in all caps, the letters dance around and I forget them very easily. This is 100 times worse for people with dyslexia.

Take "CVD" as an example. I’m doing my research for the next workshop (on accessible data visualisation, if you’re asking). And I found CVD used in articles without any explanation, as if everyone automatically knew it stands for "Colour Vision Deficiency." 

But guess what? If you search "CVD" online, the result you get on the whole of the first page is "Cardiovascular Disease." Which doesn’t fit, does it, in an article talking about colour contrast issues. 

The Brits among you may also remember the former PM (Prime Minister\! See what I did there?) David Cameron’s hilarious acronym blunder.

 

Cameron, who although relatively young, was famously not very much down with the kids, was outed as signing off his messages with “LOL”: he thought it meant “Lots of Love”, and had no idea it actually meant "Laugh Out Loud”. And who can blame him? It makes sense\! (You can blame him for a lot more though, but then again, I digress). 

So this isn’t just about ETA, or CVD, or LOL: it’s about acronyms in general. They can mean different things depending on the context, and not everyone has the same background knowledge to decode them. 

 

For people with cognitive disabilities, dyslexia, non-native speakers, or those using screen readers, acronyms can make communication harder, not easier. 

So here’s my very simple acronym advice:

* **Spell it out**: always write the full term when  using an acronym (e.g., CVD \= Colour Vision Deficiency), at least the first time, or in an easy to access footnote.   
* **Don’t assume knowledge**: unless it really is extra obvious, or you know your audience very well.  
* **Careful with all caps**: screen readers read all caps, and acronyms, letter by letter, so make sure that you only use all caps when appropriate.   
* **Don’t be lazy**: please, don't sign off with BR. It doesn't take long to write out "Best Regards" (yep, it does happen).

Accessibility is also about clear and inclusive communication: avoid lazy shortcuts for the initiated, that may leave other people behind. 

Let’s stop assuming, and start making our writing accessible for everyone.

What about you, {{contact.first\_name}}?

Have you ever been confused by unexplained acronyms?

Do you have a hilariously misunderstood acronym anecdote to share?

 

In the meantime, here's [a list of acronyms to keep you entertained](https://www.yourdictionary.com/articles/funny-acronyms).

 

Have a great rest of the week\!

 

Piccia

 

 

\*ASAP \= As Soon As Possible; AWOL \= Absent Without Leave, FUBAR \= Fucked UP Beyond All Repair

## **Thu 3 April \- Compliance vs what’s best**

### **Draft from Perplexity**

When I work with organisations to train their staff and empower them to achieve digital accessibility, they often focus on achieving a specific WCAG (Web Content Accessibility Guidelines) conformance level: typically Level AA. 

This is a practical baseline, and I understand that it makes sense. However, it also ignores a simple fact: focusing solely on compliance risks missing easy opportunities to improve accessibility in ways that benefit all users. 

Level AAA is often seen as unattainable across an entire website, but you know what? Certain criteria within this level are super-easy to achieve – and they can make a huge difference to a lot of people. 

For instance, take my last email to you: it was about acronyms, and how these useful shorthand can create barriers for many categories of users. I mentioned speakers of English as a foreign language as an example of an affected category: interestingly though, all those of you who replied enthusiastically agreeing with me, were in fact native speakers – which kind of proves my point\! 

You might also have noticed that I didn’t mention any official WCAG (Web Content Accessibility Guidelines) criteria to justify my request for clarity with acronyms. That’s because I honestly never think about compliance first. 

And also because if I had, you might have noticed that while a criterion regulating use of acronyms and abbreviations does exist, your company may not be interested in adopting it – because it’s Level AAA. Why is it Level AAA, when it’s very easy to implement, and most people would appreciate it? It beats me entirely, and I hope it gets changed. 

Here are a few Level AAA WCAG criteria that should really be A – and that you should insist your organisation implements, even if they’re only aiming for Level AA:

1. [**SC 3.1.4: Abbreviations**](https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG21/#abbreviations)  
   Provide a mechanism to identify the expanded form or meaning of abbreviations and acronyms. For example, adding tooltips or inline explanations – or, why not, simply explain in the text itself\! It’s not hard to do and it’s basic good writing practice.

2. [**SC 3.1.3: Unusual Words**](https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG21/#unusual-words)  
   Same as above, really: baseline good industry practice, but if you need further reasons, it benefits those with cognitive disabilities or those who for any reason are unfamiliar with the terminology, but do need to understand.

3. [**SC 1.4.8: Visual Presentation**](https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG21/#visual-presentation)  
   As a seasoned designer who started her career in editorial design, I can’t get over how this criterion could be Level AAA. It focuses on text readability by recommending features like limiting line lengths to 80 characters, ensuring sufficient line spacing, and avoiding justified text alignment. In a nutshell: basic good design practice – and so, so easy to achieve. 

These are not the only criteria that are inexplicably listed as AAA – but they are surprisingly easy to implement, and can make a meaningful difference.

In short, my appeal to you is: don’t focus solely on compliance. Instead, adopt an *accessibility-first* mindset. Identify and implement improvements that are easy to achieve regardless of their WCAG level, focusing on user experience rather than just compliance – with the added benefit of actually achieving even higher compliance than you were aiming for\! Isn’t that great\! 

Ignoring these "quick wins" means missing out on creating a more inclusive experience.

it can lead to overlooking simple improvements that fall outside the targeted level but significantly enhance usability. Some Level AAA success criteria, often dismissed as "too difficult," are surprisingly easy to implement and can make a meaningful difference.

## **The Problem with Compliance-Only Thinking**

Focusing solely on compliance risks missing opportunities to improve accessibility in ways that benefit all users. Level AAA is often seen as unattainable across an entire website, but certain criteria within this level are straightforward and impactful. Ignoring these "quick wins" means missing out on creating a more inclusive experience.

## **Easy Wins from Level AAA**

Here are three Level AAA success criteria that are relatively simple to achieve:

1. [**SC 3.1.4: Abbreviations**](https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG21/#abbreviations)  
    This criterion requires providing a mechanism to identify the expanded form or meaning of abbreviations and acronyms. For example, adding tooltips or inline explanations ensures clarity for users who may not understand specialized terms.

2. [**SC 3.1.3: Unusual Words**](https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG21/#unusual-words)  
    For words or phrases used in an unusual way (e.g., jargon or idioms), offering definitions or explanations can be easily done through glossaries or contextual help. This benefits users with cognitive disabilities or those unfamiliar with the terminology.

3. [**SC 1.4.8: Visual Presentation**](https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG21/#visual-presentation)  
    This criterion focuses on text readability by recommending features like limiting line lengths to 80 characters, ensuring sufficient line spacing, and avoiding justified text alignment. These adjustments enhance reading comfort for everyone, particularly users with dyslexia or visual impairments.

## **A Better Approach: Accessibility-First Mindset**

Rather than rigidly adhering to a specific WCAG level, organizations should adopt an *accessibility-first* mindset. This involves identifying and implementing improvements that are easy to achieve regardless of their WCAG level, focusing on user experience rather than just compliance.

## **Practical Steps for Broader Accessibility**

* Review all WCAG success criteria (A, AA, and AAA) and identify "low-hanging fruit" that can be implemented quickly.

* Conduct usability testing with diverse user groups, including people with disabilities.

* Regularly update content and design elements to address evolving accessibility needs.

## **Conclusion**

Accessibility is not just about meeting legal requirements; it’s about creating equitable experiences for all users. While aiming for Level AA compliance is a good baseline, consider going beyond it by incorporating simple yet impactful Level AAA criteria where feasible. By doing so, you’ll improve usability and demonstrate a genuine commitment to inclusivity.

Now all links point directly to the **WCAG success criteria themselves** on the official W3C website:

## **Tue 6 May 2025 – Lights out**

| Lights out |
| :---- |

| It was half an hour past noon more or less (12:33, as it turned out), on a Monday morning at the end of April 2025\. I was in a co-working session on Zoom, when my screens completely died on me. Oh? I thought. I was in my office, where I work on a desktop, so I had no battery to rely on.   I did what anybody would do at that point, and went on WhatsApp. The chats revealed that all of my friends, wherever they were in Valencia, had also lost power.  |
| :---- |

| That was strange enough already, as power losses are normally confined to restricted grid areas.   And then my nephew from Italy informed me that the blackout had affected the whole of Spain, plus Portugal, some areas in Southern France, and the north of Morocco...  Yes, reader: I panicked. In my defence, at this point all phone and internet connections had vanished, and I had no way of finding out what on earth was going on. Certain current geo-political situations made me fear cyber-hacking retaliation on a massive scale, something I wouldn't put past some of the evil agents working in the world today.   But I reasoned that there was nothing I could do about it, so I set off out and roamed the streets.  It was eery, and it was wonderful. It also wasn't all roses – hospitals had the worst of it, when generators didn't come on fast enough. But it was a day of lessons.    Here's what I learnt:  Never watch conspiracy theory action movies ever again (I don't as a rule, but even so, they were all replaying in my head). Always have an old-style phone, even better if rotary, plugged into your phone line (it may just work – although probably not). Re-build a record and video collection that does not rely on the internet to be viewed or listened to (the power came back, 7 hours later, but not the connection). Consider a career that does not rely on either power or comms (are there any left? Even bakeries had to close). Buy an analogue radio and a reserve of fresh batteries. Better still, build a radio station so you can transmit as well as receive. Lobby your EU representative so that the EU backs off the plan to close down all FM radio waves in favour of digital. Surely this is a lesson we have learnt??? Have some cash in your wallet. Remember that there are places in the world where this is a frequent occurrence. Be thankful for your luck. Thoughts go to those who are kept in the dark on a daily basis.  Go to the beach, and swim.   Roam the streets, and dance with strangers (there was a lot of that in Valencia, and it was brilliant).  Enjoy your day off. Make sure that your last message to your far-away family of origin group chat, before the lines cut off for 12 hours, isn't: "I am actually terrified". Lastly: we really can't function without power, peeps. Treasure your fuse box. Should lights go out for good, we'll have to re-think society.  Were you affected by the blackout?  What did you do, if so?  What do you think you would do?   In the meantime, have a happy, powerful Tuesday.   Piccia   Wed 7 May 2025 – train your staff Hey \[name\] Want your company to save money – and make money – on accessibility? There’s one very simple answer:  Train your staff. I say this to every client, and I’ll keep saying it because it’s true: if you invest in accessibility training for your team now, you’ll spend a lot less fixing problems later. And you will also open up your audience to many (and I do mean many) more people.  Yes, because it’s not just the disabled people that will finally be able to buy your products or use your services: [you’re also opening your market to their families and friends](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/kaye-moors-cpacc-02bb203_purplepound-accessibilitymatters-accessibility-activity-7325776434206072832-FGcO?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop&rcm=ACoAAAJ3Vq8BvZZ1hun3XmgIIuFxEHRCumTvXeU). A huge boost to your revenue – without a doubt. Not investing in training your teams in accessible practices, on the other hand, will mean much bigger losses.  Here’s what happens when your team doesn’t know accessibility: – They create inaccessible content – You have to rely on expensive external help – You waste time retro-fitting instead of doing it right the first time – You risk legal trouble (trust me on this one – the [European Accessibility Act](https://piccianeri.notion.site/European-Accessibility-Act-c94ebee1a6d5402cac105fa27af61f15?pvs=4) will open up the floor to this) – You shut out 25% of your audience – yes, that’s the percentage of people who have a disability at any given time – Add all of their close friends and families to that. Here’s what happens when your team does know accessibility: – They create accessible content from the start – You don’t have to call in expensive external help as often – You reach a bigger audience and make more money – You avoid legal headaches – You build a better, more inclusive brand – You turn accessibility into a huge opportunity – instead of a limitation The choice is yours.  But I implore you, please don’t wait until you get sued, lose customers, or have to rebuild everything from scratch… Take action now\! And see your revenue (as well as karma) grow, in time. 2 actions you can take now: – Check [my guide to the European Accessibility Act](https://piccianeri.notion.site/European-Accessibility-Act-c94ebee1a6d5402cac105fa27af61f15?pvs=4), and see whether it applies to your company or not: got to piccianeri dot com/eaa/ (proper link in comments) – Send me a message to chat about how I can help you turn accessibility into an opportunity, by training your teams. I can also help persuading your C-suite, if they’re still on the fence about it.  Happy Thursday, by the way\! It’s nearly the weekend\!  Email on compliance and WCAG  Perhaps before the one below which is currently scheduled to be sent on Friday 9 May in the pm – to give some value in between pitches. Actually, I’ve lost the sync between LI posts and emails. Yesterday I re-posted a really good post on WCAG not meaning accessibility, which really I should have sent as an email first. I don’t remember whether I sent it – I probably did – or when, so if I don’t remember, chances are they don’t, either.  Here goes: Bad news first: WCAG compliance does not mean actual accessibility. Huge bummer innit\! \[WCAG is the acronym for Web Accessibility Compliance Guidelines\] Here's a few reasons why: – WCAG do not consider \*all\* accessibility issues. – WCAG do not consider all best practices. – WCAG do not consider overall inclusion. – WCAG do not consider usability. And here are a few examples of bad practices that cause accessibility issues and that are ignored by the WCAG: – Use of low-contrast typefaces – Use of complicated layouts – Use of unfamiliar patterns – Use of more than one H1 – Use of too many emojis – Use of autoplay video\* \*(no, it’s not enough to provide a stop button, or to make it stop after 5 seconds as the WCAG recommend. I only need 2-3 seconds to get sick. Please no autoplay – and no GIFs in emails, I can't stop them and won't be able to read your missive) My point being: there is a big difference between providing a technically and legally compliant service, and providing a service that is truly accessible and inclusive. Stop asking the question “does it comply with WCAG?” Start asking the question “can everybody use this?” OK, that was the bad news. And here's the brilliant news\! Adopting inclusion will expand your business (and, why not, your karma too). And not by a little: by a hell of a lot. Don't believe me? I've got proof\! I've compiled a doc with recent research and data making the business case for accessibility, and demonstrating how accessibility is an unbelievably sound, long-term business strategy and growth driver – in bags of money as well as good karma. Go get your copy here: [https://lnkd.in/dGu4V86s](https://lnkd.in/dGu4V86s) It may well be what you need to finally persuade your marketing department, or that of your clients, that they should embrace accessibility for better reasons than fear of legal trouble. |
| :---- |

## **EAA bite in arse – schedule to send after the one on training the staff**

You know one thing that I hear way too often?

“The design and marketing staff are keen to learn and implement accessibility, but management aren’t sold on it yet”.

Seriously?

What is it going to take for management to work it out?

I don’t like to lead with fear, and yet here I am, reminding you if you are the boss, and reminding you to remind your bosses if you’re not the boss, that the [European Accessibility Act is about to come and bite you in the arse](https://youtu.be/wUN5fun2T0I?si=13Mzdozo-a0Rr_Fj) – very soon now (in fact, most EU legislation is already in force).

These are 3 simple questions you need to answer, in order to find out whether the EAA applies to you:

* Does your company sell online in the EU?  
* Is your company’s annual revenue over 2 million euros?  
* Does your company have more than 10 employees?

If you’ve answered “yes” to all 3 of the questions above, but your company doesn’t have an accessibility program yet: we need to talk. 

(And management doesn’t really have an option anymore). 

Should you need a reminder, as well as ammunition with your C-Suite, [here is my handy guide to the EAA](https://piccianeri.notion.site/European-Accessibility-Act-c94ebee1a6d5402cac105fa27af61f15?pvs=4). You can check there whether your company falls under any of the excluded categories. 

If it doesn’t: hit reply, and let’s talk. 

## **On Figma Sites**

### **Draft 1**

Hey \[name\]

Are you old enough to remember Photoshop 5.1?

The best version of Photoshop, ever. I carried on using it without updating for as long as I could. I just checked my shelves – and the manual I bought at the time is still there, surviving three international moves. 

At the time, designers had 3 main programs we’d use: Photoshop, Illustrator, and QuarkXpress. Photoshop was for image manipulation and anything pixel-based; Illustrator was for vector work, and QuarkXpress was for page layouts. Each of these pieces of software did a very specific job, and it did so very well. They would talk to one another, but they each knew their place.

Believe it or not, in the 90s and even beyond, designers adored Adobe. Then, somewhere along the way, Adobe got lost up their own behinds, and went on to become an out-of-touch behemoth with hyperbloated features that make no one happy. The Creative Cloud system has been defined as “*a massive bloated pile of nonsense”,* and that’s one of the kindest comments I’ve read. They basically stopped listening to thei

So when Figma came along, we all loved it: it did one job well, or at least getting there, and it 

Adobe started to grow way too much for their own good, and the programs became hyperbloated versions of themselves, often developed to carry out tasks they were not meant to do (Photoshop was not built to design web page mockups, in my humble opinion)

### **Draft 2 – sent in May**

Hey \[name\], 

Unless you’ve been under a rock, or you have nothing to do with the design world, you’re probably aware that Figma’s Config conference has just taken place, and [CEO and co-founder Dylan Field announced a whole bunch of new features](https://www.figma.com/blog/introducing-figma-sites/).

Many people have declared themselves giddy with excitement; but a whole lot of other people did not share the same level of appreciation for the news.

I don’t have the time today to explain to you why it doesn’t make me happy to see Figma go the Adobe way (I’m old enough that my favourite Photoshop version remains 5.1: still the best). 

But I am making sure I do have the time today to warn you that you should not publish your sites with Figma Sites – unless what you really want [is to create barriers for your users](https://adrianroselli.com/2025/05/do-not-publish-your-designs-on-the-web-with-figma-sites.html) and churn out a div soup that puts any page builder to shame. 

In short: the new Figma Sites web publishing platform is basically a “[horrific div generator](https://youtu.be/dS8E5FlyId8?si=QKvIvrCT3RZfz7Yp)”. 

But Piccia, I can hear you say. It’s just a beta version\! They will improve it with time\! 

Hmmm… Are we absolutely sure? 

The code generated by Figma Sites completely bypasses semantic HTML, even for basic elements such as headings.

Why oh why I am asking you, why would you deliberately choose to deploy a div with a class to output an H1, when you already have a beautifully simple semantic HTML tag for it? 

A no-code platform generates code anyway. And if it starts by ignoring the building blocks of the web – the same ones that make a product robust – I’m sorry, but I simply don’t trust it to sort that out in the future, when it’s precisely what it should have started from.

You’ve heard it from me a million times and you’ll hear it from me once more: 

It is way cheaper to start the right way, than remediate afterwards. 

Figma Sites appears to have been conceived with no regard for best practices, and at the moment it produces websites that are an embarrassing accessibility fail. 

A few very good resources that explain how and why this is happening:

## **Do Not Publish Your Designs on the Web with Figma Sites…**

by Adrian Roselli

[https://adrianroselli.com/2025/05/do-not-publish-your-designs-on-the-web-with-figma-sites.html](https://adrianroselli.com/2025/05/do-not-publish-your-designs-on-the-web-with-figma-sites.html)

A LinkedIn post with a mini- accessibility review by Emily Lawes   
[https://www.linkedin.com/posts/emilylawes\_accessibility-figma-a11y-activity-7326207820545028096-0RUN?utm\_source=share\&utm\_medium=member\_desktop\&rcm=ACoAAAJ3Vq8BvZZ1hun3XmgIIuFxEHRCumTvXeU](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/emilylawes_accessibility-figma-a11y-activity-7326207820545028096-0RUN?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop&rcm=ACoAAAJ3Vq8BvZZ1hun3XmgIIuFxEHRCumTvXeU)

And a YouTube video titled “Figma’s New Horrific Div Generator”. 

[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dS8E5FlyId8](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dS8E5FlyId8)

## **HTML**

Top accessibility tip:

Use semantic HTML – please.

Think that it doesn’t apply to you, if you’re not a developer? 

Think again. 

Do you write anything online? Yes? 

Then you can do your bit towards semantic HTML.

Last week I taught 4 classes in a digital marketing master course, and this is what I did: 

I made sure they understood what semantic HTML is, and how they should use it in their work as marketers.

This is how you can get started: 

Organise your written content using heading tags hierarchically. 

In short: one H1, followed by H2, H3, and so on. 

Tim’s email

**Pricing (and a little thank-you deal)**

A few people have asked about pricing, so here’s what’s planned:

* **Standard Price:** £195  
* **Launch Price (first month):** £145

But since you were among the first to register your interest, I wanted to say thank you with something better.

You can grab the course for just **£95** that’s over 50% off.

**The catch?** That price disappears at the end of this week. It’s a pre-launch special and won’t be repeated.

No flashy landing pages or marketing spiel  just a simple link:

![👉][image1] [https://wpsecurity101.com/secret-register-page/](https://eot.timnash.co.uk/f/a/b-ZDg9BOB5wvotUdfbsukw~~/AAAHURA~/S9nHXSnR0AXX4QzaaMRqj7CWcko_NWLZiXdTpWG4D0eUWwunD_3gyTqifCaJNz4jJU6zpAIELqR8HtOjp4QR8CiJARnEHVrGHZlTzzaVsI8veXLg8bg6OxWIT5bw5cEOHqN263PFNW4jgmedGfBEFIzvtBHkRT7XZ9ur-G02GznM5kqXE4BpKis0W31Kfk0O)

You’ll be pre-ordering the course, and you’ll be automatically enrolled as soon as it’s ready.

## **Mon 26 May**

Hey {{contact.first\_name}}

 

 

Last week [I posted on LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:share:7329756202546913281/) on one of my usual topics: how it is time for companies and agencies to get their arse in gear and start taking the European Accessibility Act seriously – you have heard this one many times before.

 

I ended the post as I tend to do: with a reminder that accessibility is an opportunity, not a limitation, and I can empower you to make sure that you make the most of embracing it.

 

Nothing new to see here.

 

But then someone made a comment about the EU restricting businesses, to which I replied that accessibility greatly expands business. And got this reply:

"I have to disagree with you. The reality is that accessibility is a cost center and it's expensive. The few percentage points of customers you can possibly gain as a result is not worth it on its own, and in many cases is just purely hypothetical. If the web is to be accessible, it will need to become so by fiat and force, not expecting companies to do it on their own."

Deep breath...

There is so much to unpack in this statement. I don't go on the internet to pick up a fight, so this was my reply:

 "Accessibility may well be expensive when a website is built disregarding it. 

 

Websites that do so, however, usually have a host of other issues, because they bypass best practices in general. 

For instance, if they ignore semantic HTML, at the basis of accessibility, or alt text for images, their SEO will also be quite bad. 

 

The best way forward is to build putting accessibility first, which results in leaner, faster websites with better SEO. 

 

As for your "purely hypothetical" assertion re: customer gain: what data do you base this on? Plenty of research shows the opposite. [I compiled some of it in this document](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1b1_VWW0Vui9hFyFx1nZnTsoxYzO1G_SDaGZSsR2NhPA/edit?usp=sharing).

 

Ultimately those who persist building inaccessible websites will keep churning out subpar products: so much of accessibility is simply best coding and design practice. And they will also be behind the times. 

 

Last but not least: the web was literally born to be accessible, see the origins of HTML. One of the creators of the web, Tim Berners-Lee said this in 1998: “The power of the web is in its universality. Access by everyone regardless of disability is an essential aspect."

 

The commenter didn't reply back.

 

Since then, I've often thought about this comment: quite revealing, in so many ways.

 

Is the opinion expressed in that comment truly what we're up against? Is this what the silent majority thinks?

 

Is this how the bosses and C-suites see accessibility?

 

Is this why this mission that I chose feels like an uphill battle at the moment?

 

What do you think, dear {{contact.first\_name}}? 

Do you agree with their take – or with my take? Or are you somewhere in between? 

Please do let me know: I’d really love to hear.

 

 

In the meantime, it's Monday: may your week be peaceful, productive, and prosperous.

 

Piccia

## **Friday 30 May 2025 – more on audits (?)**

From ChatGPT: 

You might be thinking:  
 **“An audit sounds nice, but do we really need one?”**  
 Or maybe:  
 **“Will it actually make a difference?”**

I hear you. Totally valid questions.

Here’s the truth, though: most teams wait too long before getting a proper audit. And when they finally do, they wish they’d done it sooner.

Why? 

Because audits \*work\*. 

An audit isn’t just a checklist.  
 It’s clarity. Confidence. Direction.

It’s truth bombs galore. 

 It’s the moment someone steps in and says:  
 **“Here’s what’s working. Here’s what’s getting in the way. And here’s what to do about it.”**

You get:

* A fresh perspective that sees what you’ve stopped seeing  
* Real, actionable improvements (not just problems pointed out)  
* UX, UI *and* accessibility feedback in one powerful package  
* ROI you can actually measure – like 4× conversions overnight (true story)

And right now, you get it all with **30% off**, because like I told you I just had a birthday and I’m in a generous mood. It was lovely on the beach yesterday\! And I got new swimming googles as a present, which improved my evening no end (the sun sets very late here in Spain, so there was time to test them out before we downed all the Cava).

Use code **BD30** at checkout until **Wednesday 3 June**. Or just click below and it’s applied automatically:

👉 \[Quick Wins Audit\]  
 👉 \[Accelerator Audit\]  
 👉 \[Transformation Audit\]

If you’ve been sitting on the fence, this is the sign you were waiting for.  
 Don’t miss it.

Piccia

 

As I've told you before: one day, you, too, will be over 50\.

 

Or perhaps you are already: in which case, you \*know\* that 50 is not the same as 55, which is not the same as 60, which is not the same as 65 – and so on.

 

Up to possibly into our 90s, because longevity rates are increasing, and in general we tend to keep much fitter than previous generations.

 

I am 58 (no idea how that happened, but still, way better than the alternative), and while I look after my body and take very regular exercise, I am categorically not the same as I was when merely a sprightly young 50\.

 

And I know that when I am 68, I will look back nostalgically at the me of today and I will see a bright young thing in comparison.

 

So if a fitness app asks me to select my age, but shoves me together with all other 50+, with no further nuance: I will not buy it or even try it, because **you have just shown me for a fact that I can't trust you with my wellness**. 

 

If you lump everyone above 50 in the same bucket: you have no idea how to look after our health. 

 

In fact, I'd go as far as saying that the difference between 50 and 60, or 60 and 70, is way more significant than the difference between 30 and 40\. 

 

This is obviously ageist and against good UX: so why does it keep happening? Why do we oldies constantly have to feel dismissed – when we're probably the generation with the highest spending power?

 

While I fundamentally can't know the answer for sure, I'll wager a hypothesis: 

 

Because when these apps run A/B tests, the quantitative data don't yield any significant result. 

 

So that's why I'm telling you: no, seriously, f\*\*\* quantitative data.

 

Joking: that's a sweeping statement, clearly over the top.

 

But there are cases where quantity or statistics definitely should NOT matter. And this is one of them. 

 

If it makes no difference: do the right thing. Go qualitative, all the way.

 

I've said before, and here it goes again: 

 

**Design for your future self: instead of shoving all 50+ in the same category, provide us with the same nuance everybody else gets.**

 

(this is excellent accessible design advice in general, by the way)

 

Moreover: by pissing us off with your thoughtless age ranges, you are absolutely leaving plenty of good money on the table.

 

Gen-X is re-defining what ageing means. 

 

We're climbing mountains, running marathons, smashing triathlons, skating, cycling, swimming, free diving, scuba diving, surfing, sailing, boxing, tangoing our way into our old age, and we have no intention to stop.

 

Any health or fitness-based apps or organisations ignoring this, are wasting money by the fistful.

 

We're here to stay – way longer than ageist quizzes are giving us credit for.  

 

Ageism is a thing: don't do it.

 

Thank you.

 

Rant over.

 

Happy Monday to you 

 

 

Piccia

## **Monday 2 June 2025 – Birthday offer reminder**

26 days

Hey {{contact.first\_name}}

You know what the thing on my mind today is?

Actually, there are 2 things on my mind.

 

The first one is:

67% of accessibility issues relate to design \[allegedly\].

The second one is:

There are only 26 days left until the final implementation deadline of the European Accessibility Act. 

Both could be problems – but they could also be opportunities – as I never fail to remind you.

If your company is one of the very few ones that already has accessibility under control, and even offers it as a service if you're an agency: congratulations. You already know that you are opening up to a market with huge potential: 1.9 trillion dollars in fact, I am told.

If your company has not quite got around to tackling the issue yet – and you're aware that the clock is ticking: here's my cue to remind you that you can still get one of my audits with a 30% discount (code: BD30, until Wed 4 June). 

I always point out the quick wins and the fixes, rather than just telling you what's wrong: that's part of what makes my audits so valued by my clients. They're as good as a discovery process: an audit helps delineate a roadmap, and tailor the right solution.

But if, on the other hand, your C-suite is still ignoring the very real consequences that the European Accessibility Act might have on your company or your clients, here are a few things you can do:

Share The Business Case for Accessibility with them: a doc bearing solid facts proving that the return on investment of accessibility is absolutely worth it.

Share Stéphanie Walter's article/ talk on How to Convince People to Care and Invest in Accessibility. A great digest of all the many reasons why accessibility adoption should be a no-brainer.

Share the Guide to the European Accessibility Act as a bit of a scaremongering tactic (especially the penalties section).

Share a report on ADA lawsuits in the USA, as a suitable companion piece.

Book a chat with me, to find out how I can help in your quest.

Or go straight for one of my audits, and apply the BD30 coupon (only valid until Wed 4 June).

Whatever you do, whether with me or someone else: please, do something.

I know I'm really hammering this point home relentlessly at the moment, but time is of the essence (26 days to go\!).

In the meantime, it's Monday: wishing you the best possible week ahead.

Thank you for reading me.

Piccia

## **Tuesday 3 June 2025 – “Pretty” my foot.**

| So, I usually avoid getting annoyed online – really not much point to it.   And yet, today, a LinkedIn post managed to properly irritate me.   The point of it, presumably, was that accessibility is not a checklist: it's a journey. And up to here, all good – you know that's my jam, too.   However, in order to make their point, the author made a fundamental distinction between the "functional side" – mostly controlled by code – and what they called the "pretty side".   Here's their "pretty side" list:    "Colors. Layout. Contrast ratios. Alt text on images. Design decisions."   Deep breath in Piccia. Deep breath out.   Yes. It's true: a screen reader user will not care about colours and contrast ratios.   But they absolutely \*will\* care about alt text: literally the only way they have to know what an image is about.    And they will also care about layout: content order is one of the things that can truly scramble a screen reader user experience – so it needs thoughtful design.    Moreover: there is way more to accessibility than screen readers.   There are a wide array of disabilities (neurodivergence, low vision, epilepsy, vestibular disorder, the list is long) that do absolutely rely on the "pretty side" in order to be able to use a digital application.   And if that weren't enough: remember I told you yesterday that 67% of accessibility issues stem from design? Well – I didn't make it up: I found that stat in a [Deque report](https://piccianeri.com/?ns_url=25d4&mid=119573&fch=8ed1e424).    So no: it's not true that the "pretty side" is less relevant than the "functional side".   In fact, I notice very often that clients are very keen to achieve "functional" accessibility – at the expense of "pretty" accessibility. And that's a huge mistake – please don't fall for it.   If you've ordered an accessibility audit: are you also including a visual accessibility version?   I've seen 400 pages-deep accessibility testing reports that completely ignored glaring visual and layout issues, that would significantly hinder many users' ability to get to that checkout page that was the object of the report.    So please: take the "pretty side" very seriously.    That's why I provide accessible design education: because the "pretty side" is very much foundational to achieving a product that starts from accessibility first.   And – you knew it was coming  – here's my reminder to you: there are only 25 days left until the final implementation deadline of the European Accessibility Act.    I told you that I'm running a 30% discount on [my audits](https://piccianeri.com/?ns_url=25d5&mid=119573&fch=8ed1e424) with the BD30 coupon, but I haven't told you yet that it's also applicable to my [consultancy packages](https://piccianeri.com/?ns_url=25d6&mid=119573&fch=8ed1e424).    If you're not sure what would suit you best: I am always happy to chat. [Book a call today](https://piccianeri.com/?ns_url=259b&mid=119573&fch=8ed1e424).    Whatever you do, as I always say, whether with me or anyone else: do something. It's time to take accessibility seriously – whatever the size of your company or agency.   And it starts from good design – although I might switch to just calling it "the pretty side".    Thank you, as ever, for reading me.   Piccia |
| :---- |

| p.s. These are the checkout links for the audits, with the discount applied automatically:   [The Quick Wins Audit](https://piccianeri.com/?ns_url=25cw&mid=119573&fch=8ed1e424)[The Accelerator Audit](https://piccianeri.com/?ns_url=25cx&mid=119573&fch=8ed1e424)[The Transformation Audit](https://piccianeri.com/?ns_url=25cy&mid=119573&fch=8ed1e424) |
