## **4 June 2025 – not over it yet**

Surefire way to piss off a designer:

Dismiss what we do as "the pretty side".  
   
Yep, this is a follow-up from yesterday's email: writing is my therapy and I am writing to you to get it all out of my system. And for that, I thank you.

I'm long in the tooth, so I first heard this "pretty" drivel in the 90s – with imaginative variations along the lines of "just wave your magic wand\!"  
   
Here we are, in 2025, still having to hear it now.  
   
An incomplete list (and you know that these are not my choices) of "the pretty side" and what it actually achieves for users:  
 

1. **Colours.**   
   Colours are definitely pretty. And they're also essential for accessibility: if you don't get them right, someone who is colour blind may completely miss out on that essential piece of information – or that conversion you're after.   
   Pretty – and functional ✅.

2. **Layout.**   
   Layout surely helps to make things pretty. And yet, if accessibility matters to you, it's even more important to make sure that content is in the right order both visually, and structurally, so that screen readers and other assistive tech can navigate it and make sense of it – and perhaps end up buying our shit,which is what we all want in the end, innit.   
   Layout also helps neurodivergent people to parse your pages more easily.   
   Pretty – and functional ✅.

3. **Contrast ratio.**   
   \[not sure how this ended on a "pretty side" list\].   
   People with low vision and other sight, or neurological, issues will NOT see your content if the contrast ratio is wrong. In 2025, still the overwhelming majority of sites gets this catastrophically wrong – even the biggest ones.   
   Not even up for discussion: this is functional ✅.

4. **Alt text on images.**   
   \[same as above: alt text has no place on a "pretty side" list\].  
   Let me say it again. Why was alt text on a "pretty side" list, I honestly don't know. Alt text is literally the only way a screen reader user can work out what an image is about, so: yes it can be pretty, but:   
   Not even up for discussion: this is functional ✅.

5. **Design decisions.**  
   Design decisions are, once again literally, what makes a digital product usable and accessible. Shall we use a looping animation? Background video? Legible typography? Colours? And so on. Once more, yes it can be pretty, but:   
   Not even up for discussion: this is functional ✅.

Clearer now?  
Good\! Out of my system\! Thank you for listening to my rant – it's over now.   
   
Here's to all of us designers, toiling daily on the "pretty side"\!   
Well done my friends, let's carry on making products accessible and usable, thanks to the pretty side\!   
   
And if you need help making sure that your "pretty side" is accessible from the start: remember that I provide accessible design education.   
   
And – yes, I am going there again  – here's my reminder to you: there are only **24 days left** until the final implementation deadline of the European Accessibility Act.   
   
I told you that I'm running **a 30% discount on [my audits](https://piccianeri.com/audits) with the BD30** **coupon**, and I'm reminding you again that it's also applicable to my [consultancy packages](https://piccianeri.com/consultancy) – which can be used towards education. But it runs out today\! Hurry\!   
   
If you're not sure what would suit you best: I am always happy to chat. [Book a call today](https://calendly.com/piccia/30-minute-convo).   
   
Whatever you do, as I told you yesterday and until I have breath to sustain me, whether with me or anyone else: **do something**. It's time to take accessibility seriously – whatever the size of your company or agency.  
   
And it starts from "the pretty side"\!  
   
Forever grateful to you for reading my emails.   
   
Piccia  
**p.s.**In case you're curious – but please promise to behave – here is t[he LinkedIn post that started these rants.](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/robert-mohle-jr-39961333_wcag-a11y-aoda-activity-7330792523826712576-MBmS?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop&rcm=ACoAAAJ3Vq8BvZZ1hun3XmgIIuFxEHRCumTvXeU)  
   
**p.p.s.** Once again in case it helps, here are the checkout links for the audits, with the discount applied automatically:  
   
[The Quick Wins Audit](https://piccianeri.thrivecart.com/mini-audit/?coupon=BD30)  
[The Accelerator Audit](https://piccianeri.thrivecart.com/midi-ux-ui-audit/?coupon=BD30)  
[The Transformation Audi](https://piccianeri.thrivecart.com/maxi-ux-ui-audit/?coupon=BD30)t

## **6 June 2025 – Too much freedom**

| Too much freedom |
| :---- |

| Hey {{contact.first\_name}}   Strongly held opinion: there is such a thing as too much freedom. At least in the life of a designer.    You know the saying "garbage in, garbage out"?  It means that when the brief is bad, then so is the result.   And a good brief contains well-placed constraints.     Whenever I have to design my own website, or any other personal project, the beautiful feeling of freedom is very soon replaced by blank page panic. All the options in the world\! All the choices\! Ghaaaa\! Welp...    Yes,  {{contact.first\_name}}: I need constraints. Good constraints provide guidance: that's how I always created my best work.    And that's how I look at accessibility: nothing other than an excellent brief – a good creative constraint. Especially because it forces us to look at what really matters: the user and their experience, rather than redundant details, or the designer's ego's need to be in the limelight.    This excellent article titled [The Creative Power of Constraints](https://arun.is/blog/creative-power-constraints/) brings many good examples of the role that constraints played in a number of great design projects. It's an inspiring read, and I think that you will like it, too.    Do you also like well-placed constraints? What would be your example?   In the meantime, it's Friday: may your weekend bring you rest and refreshment. I'm thinking a crisp dry white wine on the beach – or whatever is the equivalent for you.    Piccia  |
| :---- |

## **10 June 2025 – Apple’s Vista moment**

Hey {{contact.first\_name}},  
   
Apple have just announced their [new interface called Liquid Glass](https://www.apple.com/newsroom/2025/06/apple-introduces-a-delightful-and-elegant-new-software-design/).  
   
Uncharacteristically, I am at a loss for words.  
   
Meaning that it is SO bad, I don't even know where to start.   
   
I keep thinking it must be a belated April's fool – but it seems like it isn't. 18 days away from the European Accessibility Act deadline, this is what one of the giants of tech serves its public: a visually inaccessible interface, that users won't be able to opt out of.   
   
Does it look cool? Yes, if you like vintage vibes, along the lines of Windows Vista 2007\.   
   
The image at the bottom, sporting a whole set of products with the new transparent design, horrifies me: I will have a serious problem if this wretched interface truly does get deployed. I won't be able to find anything at all.  
   
And no, I don't want to use any of their amazing accessibility features, such as VoiceOver: I do have vision, but this makes it hard for anyone.   
   
Apparently there will be a "Reduce Transparency" feature, which is wrong on at least 2 counts: 

1. Never put the onus on the user to change the settings by making something inaccessible by default.  
2. I don't want to reduce it: I don't want it at all. 

I am so baffled and incredulous at this move by Apple, that I can't even get pissed off yet (and yet – wait for it when my devices update...).  
   
Meanwhile, there are quite a few hilarious posts already spreading around the web.  
   
I'll leave it here for now, with [the wise words of my friend Damian Martone](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/damianmartone_apple-just-dropped-their-new-liquid-glass-activity-7338084837200744448-9xJz?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop&rcm=ACoAAAJ3Vq8BvZZ1hun3XmgIIuFxEHRCumTvXeU):   
   
"This isn’t nitpicking:  
When Apple sets a visual trend, a lot of teams follow. And that’s the problem. Because now designers at startups and agencies are going to be asked to make it “look like Apple” even if it tanks contrast, clarity or legibility.  
   
**Accessibility isn’t a trade-off for good design. It is good design**."  
   
What's your take {{contact.first\_name}}?   
I'd love to hear what you think.   
   
Piccia 

## **25 June 2025 – I actually don’t care.**

I don't give a flying F about accessibility legislation.  
   
Or even about WCAG compliance levels.   
   
   
But Piccia, I can hear you say.

Have you not been telling us all about the European Accessibility Act (tick tock\! on 28th June\! that's **3 days away**) and its legal consequences, in Europe and beyond? 

Indeed I have\! And I will keep doing it.   
   
But the truth is that I'm doing it mostly because the only way to kick some CEOs into action is by telling them that, if they don't get their arse in gear with accessibility, they will soon be risking jail (thank you Ireland 🇮🇪 🧡 )  
   
As far as I'm concerned, inclusion trumps it all – way more than legislation. Plus, all the juicy rewards that come from opening up your audience to the disabled market, estimated to be the size of China (1.3 billions roughly)...  
   
**However, [the EAA is real](https://piccianeri.notion.site/European-Accessibility-Act-c94ebee1a6d5402cac105fa27af61f15?pvs=143), and it's coming soon.**   
   
If your C-suite are more motivated by the spectre of legal action and fines, here are a few other interesting consequences, all over the EU:

* Financial penalties start at around €60k.  
* Or on a percentage of global revenue (Spain: 5%).  
* Companies may be excluded from governmental grants.  
* Companies can report their non-compliant competitor (Germany).  
* The right to sell in the country or in the EU at large could be revoked.

Overwhelmed? Don't be.   
I know that many companies, start-ups, agencies have left it a little bit last minute.  
   
But while the deadline is true – consumers will be able to report companies directly to regulatory bodies in each country – if you know what to do, you won't get fined or thrown in jail immediately.   
   
There is a process: and having things in place such as an accessibility statement, and a demonstrable accessibility plan, would stand you in decent stead against the dreaded lawsuits, fines, and jail time.   
   
This is the actual truth: accessibility is here to stay. So it is never too late to tackle it\!  
   
Want to know how? Hit reply and let's have a chat.   
In fact, [here's my booking link to schedule a call](https://calendly.com/piccia/free-strategy-session).   
   
Hope the rest of the week is nice to you.   
   
Piccia 

## 

## **17 June 2025 – Select your age to start**

As I've told you before: one day, you, too, will be over 50\.  
   
Or perhaps you are already: in which case, you \*know\* that 50 is not the same as 55, which is not the same as 60, which is not the same as 65 – and so on.  
   
Up to possibly into our 90s, because longevity rates are increasing, and in general we tend to keep much fitter than previous generations.  
   
I am 58 (no idea how that happened, but still, way better than the alternative), and while I look after my body and take very regular exercise, I am categorically not the same as I was when merely a sprightly young 50\.  
   
And I know that when I am 68, I will look back nostalgically at the me of today and I will see a bright young thing in comparison.  
   
So if a fitness app asks me to select my age, but shoves me together with all other 50+, with no further nuance: I will not buy it or even try it, **because** **you have just shown me for a fact that I can't trust you with my wellness**.  
   
If you lump everyone above 50 in the same bucket: you have no idea how to look after our health.   
   
In fact, I'd go as far as saying that the difference between 50 and 60, or 60 and 70, is way more significant than the difference between 30 and 40\.   
   
This is obviously ageist and against good UX: so why does it keep happening? Why do we oldies constantly have to feel dismissed – when we're probably the generation with the highest spending power?  
   
While I fundamentally can't know the answer for sure, I'll wager a hypothesis:   
   
Because when these apps run A/B tests, the quantitative data don't yield any significant result.   
   
So that's why I'm telling you: no, seriously, f\*\*\* quantitative data.  
   
Joking: that's a sweeping statement, clearly over the top.  
   
But there are cases where quantity or statistics definitely should NOT matter. And this is one of them.   
   
If it makes no difference: do the right thing. Go qualitative, all the way.  
   
I've said before, and here it goes again:   
   
**Design for your future self: instead of shoving all 50+ in the same category, provide us with the same nuance everybody else gets.**  
   
(this is excellent accessible design advice in general, by the way)  
   
Moreover: by pissing us off with your thoughtless age ranges, you are absolutely leaving plenty of good money on the table.  
   
Gen-X is re-defining what ageing means.   
   
We're climbing mountains, running marathons, smashing triathlons, skating, cycling, swimming, free diving, scuba diving, surfing, sailing, boxing, tangoing our way into our old age, and we have no intention to stop.  
   
Any health or fitness-based apps or organisations ignoring this, are wasting money by the fistful.  
   
We're here to stay – way longer than ageist quizzes are giving us credit for.    
   
Ageism is a thing: don't do it.  
   
Thank you.  
   
Rant over.  
   
Happy Tuesday to you   
   
   
Piccia

## **Fri 19 September 2025**

Hey \[name\]

\[I am a sucker for getting out of my comfort zone.

Take conference talks, for instance.

Every single time, I sit stage left with my head in my hands 5 minutes before I’m due up there, rolling back and forth on my heels, and muttering to myself “why on earth did you ever think this would be a good idea”. 

Then I climb up, get into the zone and all is well again, something I can be sure about because I rehearse my talks until I am literally sick of hearing my own voice. Rehearsing ad nauseam means I can deal with anything that may come up (and come up it does). 

However, this time at Hatch conference in Berlin I did something completely different: I gave an in-person workshop. Full day 😱. To 20 people. On a totally new and very experimental topic (how to be creative and accessible, since you’re asking).\]

### **Start here:**

When I give a conference talk, I am always super prepared. 

But this year at Hatch conference in Berlin for the “Let Accessibility Work with your Creativity” full day workshop I told you about the other day, I had the opposite scenario: a solid structure and many activities, but no idea how the day would develop. Live workshops also depend a lot on the attendees: will they engage? Will they be silent? Will they expect to be led?

This crowd was great: they engaged fully, and the session was even livelier than I’d hoped. 

The highlight of the day? This might come as a surprise: it was the videos by disabled testers Dave and Sam (with many thanks to Fable), interacting – or failing to interact – with some award-winning websites abounding in creativity, but not so much in accessibility. 

So much to learn there, also in terms of patience: my irritation threshold with tech that doesn’t behave is waaaay lower than Sam’s or Dave’s. Which also says a lot about what both of them have to put up with on a daily basis… 

And you know what was also fascinating? 

How drastically different Dave’s and Sam’s reactions were to the same website. Dave is tetraplegic and uses an alternative navigation device, while Sam is blind and uses a screen reader. 

What Dave considered cool was invisible to Sam. 

What Sam found interesting was not even experienced by Dave.

For instance, some of the sites we examined used auto-play sound – yep they did, as if it were 2005 (or 1999?). Of course us UX purists recoil in horror. 

But Sam, after struggling to find out how to turn the sound off – spoiler: he didn’t really manage – came out with the mind-blowing observation that using sound could open up really creative opportunities for screen reader users.

Knock me down with a feather\!

I could tell you more, but in the interest of keeping a long story short: whatever you do, get your bosses or clients to give you budget for disabled testers. There is nothing like seeing how someone uses your product in ways that you hadn’t possibly imagined – or how they fail to achieve tasks you’d taken for granted.

My Accessible Design and Content course will contain many examples of disabled testers interacting with websites and apps, to keep our awareness and empathy alert and on edge, and to make sure we start designing truly while keeping \*everyone\* in mind. 

In the meantime, it’s Friday: have a wonderful weekend.

Piccia

\[Moreover, going to a conference to deliver an intimate experience to just 20 people is the polar opposite of being under the spotlight. After the event, only a handful of people will recognise you and want to talk to you. As a consequence, my second time at Hatch was much less overwhelming than a conference usually is: the focus this time was on fewer, longer, more meaningful conversations, rather than a lot of shorter ones that all become a blurry fog after a while.\] 

And that’s 

### **Knock me down with a feather – final email**

When I give a conference talk, I am always super prepared down to the minute.

But this year at Hatch conference in Berlin for the [“Let Accessibility Work with your Creativity”](https://piccianeri.com/?ns_url=25b2&mid=136460&fch=ca851f35) full day workshop I told you about the other day, I had the opposite scenario: clear learning outcomes, a solid structure and many activities, but no real idea how the day would develop. It was the first time I'd deliver it, and the topic lends itself to being extremely experimental. So I decided I'd go that way – in pure Berlin style. 

Live workshops also depend a lot on the attendees: will they engage? Will they be silent? Will they expect to be led?

This crowd was great: they engaged fully, and the session was even livelier than I’d hoped.   
The highlight of the day? This might come as a surprise: it was the videos by disabled testers Dave and Sam (with many thanks to [Fable](https://piccianeri.com/?ns_url=25eo&mid=136460&fch=ca851f35)), interacting – or failing to interact – with some award-winning websites abounding in creativity, but not so much in accessibility. 

So much to learn there, also in terms of patience: my irritation threshold with tech that doesn’t behave is waaaay lower than Sam’s or Dave’s. Which also says a lot about what both of them have to put up with on a daily basis…   
And you know what was also fascinating? 

How drastically different Dave’s and Sam’s reactions were to the same website. Dave is tetraplegic and uses an alternative navigation device, while Sam is blind and uses a screen reader. 

What Dave considered cool was invisible to Sam.   
What Sam found interesting was not even experienced by Dave.

For instance, some of the sites we examined used auto-play sound – yep they did, as if it were 2005 (or 1999?). Of course us UX purists recoil in horror. 

But Sam, after struggling to find out how to turn the sound off – spoiler: he didn’t really manage – came out with the mind-blowing observation that using sound could open up really creative opportunities for screen reader users.

Knock me down with a feather\!

I could tell you more, but in the interest of keeping a long story short: whatever you do, get your bosses or clients to give you budget for disabled testers. [Fable](https://piccianeri.com/?ns_url=25eo&mid=136460&fch=ca851f35) is a great option. There is nothing like seeing how someone uses your product in ways that you hadn’t possibly imagined – or how they fail to achieve tasks you’d taken for granted.

My Accessible Design and Content course will contain many examples of disabled testers interacting with websites and apps, to keep our awareness and empathy alert and on edge, and to make sure we start designing truly while keeping \*everyone\* in mind. 

In the meantime, it’s Friday: have a wonderful weekend. 

Piccia

## **Tue 23 Sept 2025**

### **Accessible guide to Valencia**

Do you have a side hustle? 

If you don’t, and you work in tech: I suggest you start an “[inexpensive experiment](https://rbefored.com/exploring-new-work-paths-with-inexpensive-experiments-8748e4fc7a3e)” sooner rather than later – but that’s another email.

I have one that I recently started “planting seeds” for, and as that will end in a guide to Valencia, where I live, it also miraculously kind of ties in with my BA and MA degrees in Art History – so nice to use it again\! I’d missed it. 

As part of that side hustle, I’m in the process of visiting all the attractions in the city. 

Off on my bike I go, touring around, taking photos and videos, and expanding my knowledge on the history, art, and culture of the place. It could get worse, as far as side hustles go – also considering it involves quite a bit of sampling local delicacies.

And because of what I do, I started asking a number of questions wherever I go. Along the lines of:

* Is this place accessible on a wheelchair or a mobility scooter?  
* How close can you get by car?  
* Is there an audio guide?  
* Is the audio guide descriptive?  
* Is there a written alternative to the audio guide?  
* Are there Braille captions?  
* Are there large text captions?

Then you start looking at the exhibits and this is what tends to happen:

* Captions on the walls miles away from the actual exhibit.  
* Hard to get close to and at an awkward height.  
* In a tiny, thin typeface on a dark background.  
* In a dimly lit room.  
* Often assuming specific knowledge.

So that’s where I had the light bulb moment, and to be frank the real surprise is that it took me this long to switch this particular light on. 

What if, alongside the usual "must-see" recommendations, it included the accessibility details that actually matter? The real-world information about whether you can actually enjoy a place, regardless of how you experience the world.

I'm imagining sections like "Getting There" (actual parking distances, step-free routes), "What You'll Find" (lighting conditions, text sizes, audio options), and "Making the Most of It" (the best times to visit for different needs, alternative ways to engage with exhibits).

Because in actual fact: while compliance checkboxes are needed, accessibility is actually about creating experiences that work for everyone. And if I'm already exploring every corner of this beautiful, multi-layered, ever-surprising city, why not document it in a way that helps more people actually get out there and explore it too?

What is staggering, actually, is the fact that as far as I know, your average guide usually doesn’t include any kind of information on the accessibility of a tourist attraction.

Can you imagine having to call in advance every single time? How much longer would it take you to organise the simplest trip?

All it takes is a change of perspective: start putting yourself in other people’s shoes, and experiencing the world from their point of view. That’s when you start noticing whether that ramp is too steep, or those captions are unreadable. 

That’s exactly the mindset shift I teach in my Accessible Design and Content course: the course I wish had existed when I started learning about accessibility – coming to your screens very very soon.

Stay tuned for updates, and if you are planning to visit Valencia, let me know and I’ll pass on the Insta account details. 

In the meantime: it’s only Tuesday\! All sorts of beautiful things can still happen this week\! And may they all happen to you and your loved ones. 

Piccia 

## **Fri 27 Feb 2026 – Overlays**

Hey,

When I was a child, I’d say 6 or 7 years old, I got invited to a birthday party – as you do. 

Once I got there, the mother of the birthday child flattered me by telling me that I was the Chosen One for this wonderful surprise game that would get me a present at the end, as well as help make her child’s birthday a truly special one. My “Yes\!” was loud and enthusiastic. 

Upon acceptance, I was blindfolded. The aim of the game was for me to be led to the end of an obstacle course while unable to see where I was going, only following verbal instructions. 

A great prize would await me and all of the party upon successful completion: I was the anointed saviour of the whole party.

So there I was, blindfolded on the lawn, listening to this woman's instructions to get to the end of this obstacle course, while dozens of children were shouting at the top of their lungs, either guiding me or actively trying to get me off course.

And then, the screams get frantic. The mother is urging me on, yelling Yes\! Yes\! You're there Piccia\! You're so great\! Just one step now… JUST ONE MORE STEP\!

... I took that step. Because I trusted this woman blindly.

And that last step downwards, that I took with blindfolded faith, plunged me, fully clothed, straight into a paddling pool full of water. Fully clothed and blindfolded.

The noise went even louder – unbearable.

It was the sound of ignominy.

It was the sound of 30 children hysterically laughing at me.

They'd been anticipating this moment in a frenzied crescendo, inevitably leading to my planned burning humiliation.

Who was laughing?

Everyone else but me.

This, my friends, is what Deceptive Design is.

On the web, what this evil monster of a woman did to gullible little me would be called Bait and switch.

The user sets out to do one thing, but a different, undesirable thing happens instead. In some cases, as undesirable as being plunged into a pool while fully clothed and blindfolded, thinking you’re about to be rewarded with glory and a splendid gift.

**And this is exactly what accessibility overlays do.** 

Overlays are website widgets that provide visitors with options to modify the site's user interface.

This, if you don’t know any better, may seem like an excellent application of the 2nd principle of Universal Design: Flexibility in use. 

The core idea of this principle, as I was telling the students of the Accessible Design and Content course during session 12 this week, is that people can use the design in ways that work for them.

Simple, no? 

No. 

Because overlays are evil. 

But you would never think that when you look at an overlay website.

Overlays simply put an extra layer on top of a website’s tech stack, which can often make it harder – when not downright impossible – for users of assistive tech to access the UI. 

But the average company that’s looking for an accessibility solution would never guess that, when they read their sales copy. 

All of these tools claim that they will solve your accessibility issues, with zero effort on your part. 

But it isn’t true.  

In 2025 UsableNet reports that over 1,000 businesses were sued for web accessibility violations even though they were using an overlay. 

Also in 2025, accessiBe was fined one million dollars by the US Federal Trade Commission, as compensation to their users who’d been duped into believing their accessibility was sorted – only to get sued by their own users. 

The overlay UserWay was also sued last year, for similar reasons: they’ve been trying to get the case thrown out of court, but I’m happy to report that they have, so far, failed. 

In the EU, German auditors have declared that the use of accessibility widgets disqualifies websites from accessibility certifications. 

I could carry on but I won’t.

If your company, or your company’s clients, use an overlay, or express the intention of doing so: tell them NO. 

They are the worst bait and switch ever. 

Happy Friday to you\! 

P.s hilariously, accessiBe’s homepage features an unstoppable animation: a blatant failure of **WCAG 2.1 Success Criterion 2.2.2 (Pause, Stop, Hide)**. [https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/\#pause-stop-hide](https://www.w3.org/WAI/WCAG22/quickref/#pause-stop-hide) 

## **Fri 27 March 2026**

Hey {{contact.first\_name}},

 

A myth that needs to be debunked:

You can only get the best Italian pizza in Naples.

 

Well, sure. You get fantastic pizza in Naples (have you not been yet? I highly recommend it – both the city and the pizza pies), mostly because mozzarella comes from southern Italy and you haven't really ever had mozzarella, or burrata, until you go to southern Italy and savour the real thing. Absolute bliss – can't even explain. So any pizza with fresh mozzarella on it, straight from the local dairy farm, has an unfair advantage over any other pizza. 

 

But the best Italian pizza I ever had was in London. Despite the long journey the mozzarella had to embark on. Granted: the pizzeria was 100% Neapolitan, and they did have a wood oven of course – more essential than mozzarella.

 

I could carry on talking about pizza for ages, but there's another myth I want to debunk for you today:   
You can fix an inaccessible PDF after the fact.

Well, sure. You can add tags. You can manually set the reading order. You can spend a deeply tedious afternoon in Acrobat trying to untangle a document that was born without a thought to disabled people.

Or you can do the work in Word before you export.

Which I cannot stress enough is the better approach.  
If you've been around for a while, you've heard this from me before: just start with accessibility.

Now I know what some of you might be thinking: Word? I'm a designer. I live in Figma (and Claude...).

And yet: proposals, briefs, reports, guidelines, handover docs. We all write them. And a lot of them end up as PDFs at some point, or HTML, if you're publishing to the web. Either way, what happens in Word usually doesn't stay in Word.

So: here's what happens when you export a Word document to PDF (or HTML, for that matter).

If your headings are real headings (using Word's built-in styles, not manually bolded text), they become properly tagged headings in the export. If your images have alt text in Word, they carry that alt text through. If your tables have a proper header row marked as such, the structure is inherited. If your reading order in Word is logical, the export follows it.

But if none of the above is true: the output will be, in the technical sense, a disaster. The tags will be wrong or absent. The reading order will be chaotic. A screen reader will read it in the order the elements were added to the document, which is almost never the order they appear on the page.

The moral of the story is not "learn Acrobat", or "only have pizza in Naples".   
The moral, and this applies to everything, not just Word, is: do it right at the beginning. Be it pizza, or a digital document. 

 

Every single accessibility decision made upstream saves hours of remediation downstream. 

And it's free. Not only free: it will save you heaps of time and money later. Plus: most "accessibility decisions" are in fact, very simply, best practice decisions. With this cherry tomato on the pizza: your content will be much clearer and easier to digest.

However, for the "accessibility is expensive" brigade: making a Word document accessible costs exactly the same as making it inaccessible. While making it inaccessible will cost you dearly, in time and in remediation money.

This "do it right at the beginning" principle, by the way, runs through everything I teach. It's the spine of the comprehensive accessibility course I'm currently building (and that some of you have already bought\! We meet on Tuesdays for live rehearsals).

 

Don't know how to get Word right? I've got you covered.  
I've put together an Accessible Word document checklist: the same one I give my workshop clients. I'm planning to turn this into a paid asset at some point, as part of a bigger suite, so now is the time to get it for free.

Just reply to this email and I'll share the link with you.

 

In the meantime: it's Friday again\!

May your weekend bring you all you desire from it. 

 

Piccia

| Friday 3 April 2026 – Human nature (on WebAIM million report 2026\)  Hey Test,   Focusing on improvement is part of human nature. We all want to get better at what we do: we all want our latest output to be better than the previous.   Whether you're a writer, a barista, a musician, a chef, a teacher, a carpenter, a cleaner, a designer: whatever your field of human endeavour, it is human to want to make sure we keep getting better, to keep producing better results as we evolve. So when I tell you that web accessibility got measurably worse this year, it is definitely with a heavy heart and a crying rivers emoji.   ![😭][image2] Every year, WebAIM (Web Accessibility In Mind, an accessibility research organisation based at Utah State University) analyses the home pages of the top one million websites and checks them for accessibility errors.   This year's results, recently published, are the worst in recent memory.The average number of detectable accessibility errors per page has gone up by more than 10% in a single year: from 51 errors per page in 2025 to 56.1 in 2026\.   The share of home pages with detected WCAG (Web Content Accessibility Guidelines) failures has gone back up too, from 94.8% to 95.9%, reversing six consecutive years of slow but real improvement.   ![😭][image3]![😭][image4]![😭][image5] And alas\!  the most common failures are the same six as always: low contrast text missing alt text missing form labels empty links empty buttons missing document language. These six categories account for 96% of all detected errors, and they have not changed since the study began in 2019\. Seven years. The same six problems. Still not fixed.   (That's why I still give workshops and [talks on colour](https://piccianeri.com/?ns_url=25h2&mid=154093&fch=bd6120b7) by the way. I will carry on until it's sorted) The report points to some probable causes: increased page complexity, over-reliance on third-party JavaScript libraries...   But, most important of all for a lot of us, the most likely main culprit is what  we all affectionately know as "vibe coding". That is to say, using AI tools to generate code quickly, accepting the output, and shipping it without checking whether it actually works for everyone. The promise of all vibe coding tools is speed. Take your design and your prompts, and produce a website or an app from it. It's like a miracle\! Look mummy, I've built an app in an afternoon\!    But the reality, too often, is a disastrous div soup with no semantic structure, no landmark regions, no heading hierarchy, images with no alt text, and 300 lines of markup for a simple button: the full catalogue of WebAIM's greatest hits, generated automatically and at scale.   But, I can hear you say. Piccia, we need to use AI or it will take our jobs\! It's become unprofessional to not use AI\! Yes. I agree. It has almost become unprofessional to ignore AI in the field of design. I use Claude to help me design and build things. Of course.    But, and this is crucial, I know enough to brief Claude properly: I know what accessible HTML looks like, I know to ask for semantic structure, proper heading levels, descriptive link text, labelled inputs, colour contrast. I know to check the output, and I also know when something has gone wrong.  Are my results perfect? Nope. There is no such thing as perfection when it comes to me, and also when it comes to accessibility.   But my results are still a damn sight better than if I left Claude – or, shudder\! Figma Sites – churn stuff out without supervision. So my output this year is better than it was last year, because I try, oh my gawd do I try, to keep improving rather than going backwards.   Which is precisely why I do what I do: bespoke workshops, consultancy, and the comprehensive accessible design and content course I'm currently building (hello to those of you already in it: see you Tuesday\!). All of this keeps me learning and hopefully getting better, instead of worse.   I'm not saying that you should sign up to work with me or buy my course (although you can if you want to): I'm just saying, if there's anything we can learn from the WebAIm Million 2026 is that we should all try and reverse the trend, and make sure we keep advancing in our march towards inclusion, rather than regressing.   So whether you want to learn with me or with someone else: let's keep vibe coding, but let's make sure we train the tools to do it with accessibility in mind. Let's keep moving forwards, instead of backwards.    Can we make that promise to ourselves? In the meantime, here's the full [WebAIM Million 2026 report](https://piccianeri.com/?ns_url=25h3&mid=154093&fch=bd6120b7) if you want to read it yourself.     Oh and, happy Easter if you celebrate\! I'm truly looking forward to the break. I hope you get your chance to recharge in the next few days, too.     Piccia     |
| :---- |

| P.s. I mentioned Figma Sites because it's a designer favourite but it truly is bad, probably worse than you think. If you want a measure of how bad, check out this [article by Adrian Roselli on Figma Sites](https://piccianeri.com/?ns_url=25br&mid=154093&fch=bd6120b7). |
| :---- |

## **Friday 17 April 2025 – alt text length**

### **First draft from Claude:**

Hey {{contact.first\_name}},

 

Pesky alternative text: it should be one of the easiest 

A student on the Accessible Design and Content course asked me a question this week that I want to share with you, because I suspect it will resonate with many of you.  
He is a UX/UI designer working on a museum redesign that must comply with WCAG (Web Content Accessibility Guidelines) AA. His question was about alt text length for artworks: the W3C says alt text should be "the most concise description possible", and the WAVE accessibility checker flags a warning if you go over 100 characters. But 100 characters cannot begin to describe a painting. So what do you do?  
Here is my answer.  
The 100-character alert in WAVE is a heuristic, not a WCAG rule. It is flagging something worth investigating, not a violation. WCAG success criterion 1.1.1 does not specify a character limit. "Concise" is relative to the complexity and purpose of the image. For a diagram, a sentence may be plenty. For an artwork, it almost certainly is not.  
Artworks are not just complex images: they are a category of their own. A painting carries historical, visual, emotional, and symbolic information all at once. The underlying principle of accessibility is that everyone should have the same experience, regardless of ability. So ask yourself: would a sighted person visiting this museum page get a rich, meaningful encounter with this work? Then a blind or low-vision user deserves exactly the same.  
That means the alt text needs to actually describe the image: the composition, the figures, the palette, the mood, the technique. Think of it as describing the painting to someone over the phone, someone who cannot see it and is relying entirely on your words. The artist's name and the date, by the way, almost certainly belong in the caption, not the alt text: a caption is visible text that adds context for everyone, whereas alt text is a replacement for the visual experience itself. Putting "Portrait by Zurbarán, 1650" in the alt text and nothing else leaves a blind user with a label, not a description.  
For a genuinely complex work, use both alt text and a long description, but treat them as doing different jobs. The alt text gives the essential visual description: what is in the image, what it looks like, what the dominant impression is. The long description goes further into iconography, symbolism, and historical context, as much as the work demands.  
And it does not have to be a hidden technical field. The best museum websites surface long descriptions as visible text, as an expandable "image description" section, or as part of the artwork's information panel. That benefits everyone: blind and low-vision users, yes, but also sighted users who want to look more closely, people with cognitive disabilities, people whose first language is not the language of the wall label, and anyone who wants to understand what they are looking at. Good image description is good curatorial writing.  
One more thing, and I say this as an art historian as much as an accessibility specialist: writing descriptions for artworks is a genuine skill. There are trained audio describers, and there are writers who specialise in it. If a museum has a significant collection, it is worth making the case for bringing in that expertise, or at the very least developing a consistent internal guide. The skill does not come automatically, even to people who know art deeply.  
Piccia  
P.S. Questions like this are exactly what the Accessible Design and Content course is for. If you work in a team that creates or commissions digital content, it might be exactly what you need.

### **Final version, including the convo with Alice:** 

Hey {{contact.first\_name}},

Like I told you last week: pesky alt text\! It seems like it should be so easy, but when you get started on it in a real-world scenario, it is anything but.

And here's a real-world scenario, featuring very legitimate alt text doubts:

One of the students on the Accessible Design and Content course is  a UX/UI designer, working on a museum website re-design that must comply with WCAG (Web Content Accessibility Guidelines) Level AA.

Their question was about alt text length for artworks: the W3C says alt text should be "the most concise description possible", and the WAVE accessibility checker flags a warning above 100 characters.

But 100 characters cannot even begin to describe a painting.

So what do you do?

First of all: the 100-character limit in WAVE is a rough heuristic, not a rule. WCAG success criterion 1.1.1  (non-text content) specifies no character limit whatsoever. "Concise" is relative to the complexity and purpose of the image.

Second of all: this is also a healthy reminder that – and I know you’ve heard me say this before if you’ve been around a while – automated checkers always need a manual check; WCAG are only guidelines; and simply following them will not guarantee inclusion. 

Third of all, and most importantly, here’s another healthy reminder of the fundamental principle that should guide every single one of our decisions on accessibility in general: strive to give everyone the same experience, regardless of ability.

For artworks, that means the alt text needs to actually describe the image. Think of it as describing the painting to someone over the phone, old style, not a video call, someone who maybe wants to buy it, but who cannot see it and is relying entirely on your words.

What is in the image?

What does it look like?

What is the dominant impression?

![][image6]

| See the example above, a landscape painting by Jean-François Raffaëlli, titled Montmartre in the Snow (1880-1890), from the Museum of Bellas Artes in Bilbao. The short version: "A painting of a snow-covered urban square in winter, almost entirely rendered in white, grey, and pale beige. Two bare trees dominate the foreground on the left, their dark branches reaching into an overcast sky. Small dark figures cross the open ground in the middle distance, and a corner building with snow-dusted rooftops closes the right side of the composition. The mood is quiet and still." That is the alt text: enough to give someone a real sense of the image, but not exhaustive (yet, most definitely above 100 characters\!). You want people who use screen readers to be able to “scan” the painting rapidly and get an idea, the way people with vision do. Screen reader users all have jobs\! Chores\! Stuff to do\! Let’s make sure they get the gist of the image, but let’s not burden them with a super long description. On the other hand, the long description, which lives elsewhere on the page or in an expandable section, can go deeper into technique, palette, composition, and historical context. Now, there are published guidelines on this. My friend [Alice Orrù](https://piccianeri.com/?ns_url=25ha&mid=156194&fch=61f65f53), an inclusive communication consultant who managed alt text and long descriptions for an Italian museum website rebuild that I directed, reminded me of the [Cooper-Hewitt Museum's guidelines](https://piccianeri.com/?ns_url=25hb&mid=156194&fch=61f65f53), which are genuinely thoughtful, helpful, and worth reading.  However\!   We both have reservations about one of their key examples of alt text for a portrait of a well-known public figure: "Portrait of former First Lady Michelle Obama seated looking directly at us."   Could you conjure up anything about this image – if you had never "seen" Michelle Obama?  That tells us nothing about the image, or the person in it: the palette, the composition, the dress, the gaze, the background. It also assumes prior knowledge of what Michelle Obama looks like – and it assumes a US audience (there are many other first ladies and husbands in the world\!).   A blind user who is unfamiliar with Obama, or who simply wants to experience this specific painting on its own terms, without having to delve into the rest of the page content to get to the long description, gets almost nothing.   The long description in the same guidelines is excellent: but the alt text, by their own admission a "sentence fragment of approximately fifteen words", falls short of the equivalence principle.   Let’s be fair to blind users\! Let’s give them as close an experience as possible to the one sighted users have.    One final thing for today, and I say this as a trained art historian as much as an accessibility specialist: writing descriptions for artworks is a genuine skill.   There are trained audio describers, and there are writers who specialise in it (like Alice Orrù). If a museum has a significant collection, it is worth making the case for bringing in that expertise, or at the very least developing a consistent internal guide. The skill does not come automatically, even to UX designers, or to people who know art deeply. Is alt text starting to become easier for you? Or is it still a bit of a pain in the arse?  In the meantime, it’s Friday\! Have yourself the best weekend ever.  Piccia |
| :---- |

| P.s. This kind of question comes up constantly in the [The Accessible Design and Content course](https://piccianeri.com/?ns_url=25h6&mid=156194&fch=61f65f53).  If you work in a team that creates or commissions digital content, it might be exactly what you need. |
| :---- |

|  |
| :---- |

## **Tuesday 21 April 2025**

Draft from Claude which most definitely needs re-writing:

Hey {{contact.first\_name}},

When I was working as a creative director, the briefs I dreaded most were the ones that said: total creative freedom. Do whatever you want. Surprise us.

The ones I loved were the ones hedged with constraints: the budget, the format, the deadline, the brand, the audience, the regulatory requirements. Give me a fence and I will make the garden inside it extraordinary. Take the fence away and I stare at the horizon, paralysed by infinite possibility.

I have been thinking about this a lot lately, because I have just been invited to speak at UX London 2026\.

I want to be precise about the word "invited": I did not apply. They reached out to me.

I will not pretend I didn't do a little dance around my kitchen.

UX London is one of the most prestigious conferences in our field, and being approached to speak there is the kind of thing you do not take for granted. I am genuinely honoured, and more than a little thrilled.

The talk I will be giving is called The best creative brief, and it sits right at the intersection of the two things I care about most: accessibility and creativity. The argument, in short, is this: accessibility requirements are not the enemy of creative work. They are a brief. And a good brief, as any honest creative will tell you, is where the best work begins.

I gave a version of this talk at WeyWeyWeb in Málaga last autumn, and the response told me it needed a wider audience. Now it will get one.

More details to follow as we get closer. In the meantime: if you work with, manage, or are a designer or creative who still believes that accessibility and creativity pull in opposite directions, this might be the talk to send them to.

Piccia

P.S. Everything I share with this list comes here first, before it goes anywhere else. So you already knew before LinkedIn did.

### **Final:**

Hey {{contact.first\_name}},

Have you ever been to London? If you have, you were probably advised to visit Shoreditch: described as the “hub for creativity and artistic expression”; “dynamic playground”; “buzzing nightlife”; “best food spots”; “trend-makers capital”; and so on. 

Rewind to the late 90s and early 2000s:

Shoreditch was an urban wasteland very few brave souls dared to venture into. 

Anyone who moved there in those years was considered a pioneer. We’d occasionally visit an artist friend who lived by Shoreditch Church in a real loft (not the pretend ones of later years, this one was an ex-factory, cavernous, windy, and gelid), and we had to bring provisions in from more civilised areas: even getting milk for coffee from the corner shop was treated as a mission probably requiring a flak jacket (there was no corner shop in the vicinities in fact).  

I remember bidding an internal farewell to a close friend who moved to the Hackney road – a bit farther east than Shoreditch – around 1996, thinking “adieu dearest Claire\! We’ll never see you again\!” (ignore the drama – we did). 

Ah, the stories\! East London in those years… a never-ending adventure. 

Of course, very shortly after I moved to East London, too. I, and all the rest of my peers who surrounded me, were propelled forwards by an irresistible tide of vibrant innovation, fun, excitement, washing us all en masse towards Shoreditch and beyond. Those were the good years in London and the UK, and I’m privileged to have lived them. 

So when an East-London based conference such as UX London invites me to speak, I jump at the chance.

Yes\! I will be speaking at UX London, in Shoreditch, on 3 June 2026\. Yay\! Beyond delighted to be in The Smoke again – this time on stage.

UX London is one of the most prestigious conferences in our field, and being approached to speak there is the kind of thing you do not take for granted. I am genuinely honoured and grateful, and more than a little thrilled. Chuffed to bits, in fact\! 

The talk I will be giving is called The best creative brief, and it sits right at the intersection of the two things I care about most: accessibility and creativity. 

The argument, in short, is this: accessibility requirements are not the enemy of creative work. They are a brief. And a good brief, as any creative designer worth their Copic markers will tell you, is where the best work begins.

You might remember, if you’ve been around here a while, that I gave a version of this talk at WeyWeyWeb in Málaga last autumn, and the response told me it needed a wider audience. Now it will get one – in my favourite city in the world\!  

Where do you live, {{contact.first\_name}}? I’ve been in Spain for the past 10 years, and I’m very happy here – but I will always identify as a Londoner. 

In the meantime, it’s Tuesday\! Almost half the way through. Have a productive and satisfactory rest of your week. 

Piccia 

## **Friday 24 April 2026 – Alt text vs captions**

### **Draft from Claude:**

Last week I told you about alt text for artworks, and the equivalence principle: give everyone the same experience, regardless of ability.

This week, a follow-up question that comes up every single time I teach alt text: what about captions? Are they the same thing? Do you need both? If you write a caption, can you skip the alt text?

Short answer: they are not the same thing, and they serve completely different purposes.

Alt text is invisible in normal use. It lives in the document or page structure, it is read by screen readers, and it is displayed by browsers when an image fails to load. The rest of the time, nobody sees it. It is written for people who cannot see the image: screen reader users, people on slow connections, search engines.

A caption is visible text, placed near the image, read by everyone including screen reader users. It is not a description of what the image shows. It adds something the image alone does not say: where it was taken, when, by whom, or why it matters in this specific context.

Here is where people get tangled up. If you have written a caption that fully describes the image, repeating all of it in the alt text is redundant and actively annoying for screen reader users, who will hear the same information twice. In that case, the alt text can be shorter, or can simply reference the caption. But it should still exist: screen reader users can navigate by images, and a completely empty alt attribute tells them nothing useful.

If there is no caption, the alt text carries the full descriptive load. That is when you need the level of detail we talked about last week.

One practical thing, especially if you work in Word: captions must be inserted using the built-in mechanism. Right-click the image and select Insert Caption, or go to References \> Insert Caption. A line of text you type manually below an image has no structural relationship to the image. A screen reader will not treat it as a caption, and it will not carry through correctly when you export to PDF or HTML.

The source document must be right before any exported format can be accessible. Which is a principle that applies far beyond captions.

Is alt text starting to feel more manageable? Or are there still scenarios where you're not sure what to do? Hit reply and let me know: I genuinely read every response, and your questions often become future emails.

Have a great weekend.

Piccia

P.S. The Accessible Design and Content course covers all of this in depth, with real examples and exercises. If your team creates or commissions digital content, it might be exactly what you need.

### **My version:**

The equivalence principle: 

Give everyone the same experience, regardless of ability.

So if you have an image on a page, you must also describe it for those who can’t see it. And that’s alt text (as described in my email to you last Friday, 17 April 2026).

But what about captions though? 

Are they the same thing? 

Do you need both? 

If you write a caption, can you skip the alt text?

Sorry to disappoint: nope, they are not the same thing, and they serve completely different purposes.

Alt text is invisible in normal use. It lives in the document or page structure, it is read by screen readers, and it is displayed by browsers when an image fails to load. The rest of the time, nobody sees it (unless you’re on Bluesky, where you can click on the ALT tag on an image, and read the alt text. I recommend you do it\! Sometimes, the alt text is even better than the post, or the image on its own). 

Alt text, I’ll remind you, is written for people who cannot see the image: screen reader users, people on slow connections, search engines.

So, what's a caption for? 

A caption is visible text, placed near the image, read by everyone including screen reader users. It is not a description of what the image shows – rather, it adds something the image alone does not say: where it was taken, when, by whom, why it matters in this specific context, or other information of interest.

And here, my friend, is where most people get tangled up. 

If you have written a caption that fully describes the image, repeating all of it in the alt text is redundant and actively annoying for screen reader users, who will hear the same information twice. 

But that’s because you’ve misunderstood the role of a caption.

A caption assumes that you know what’s in the image. It is meant to add further information that you cannot necessarily infer from the image itself. 

Jean-François Raffaelli, Montmartre sous la neige, Montmartre in the snow, 1880-1890, Museo de Bellas Artes, Bilbao, Spain.

For instance, in the case of the painting we looked at last week, the caption should contain essential information, such as the author, the title, the year, and so on, that you wouldn't know just by looking at the image – unless you were an art historian specialising in that artist, or if you worked in the museum and walked past that painting a few times a day.

 

Or in the case of a portrait of former US First Lady Michelle Obama, the caption may say: "The US First Lady Michelle Obama in 2009, wearing the inaugural ball gown designed by Jason Wu. Obama became known for using her fashion choices to support young, diverse, and independent designers".

The difference between alt text and caption is in the top 5 questions I get asked. So I hope this helps\!  

Do you have any other alt text or caption questions? Hit reply and ask away.

In the meantime, it’s Friday, once again\! And once again, this is my opportunity to wish you a refreshing weekend, doing the things that you like, with the people you like. 

Have a great weekend.

Piccia

P.S. The Accessible Design and Content course covers all of this in depth, with real examples and exercises. If your team creates or commissions digital content, it might be exactly what you need.

## **Tuesday 28 April 2026 – language**

### **Claude’s draft (not sent yet):** 

Hey {{contact.first\_name}},

Here's something that has happened to me more than once.

I use a term. Someone politely tells me it's outdated. I update my vocabulary and move on. Then, some time later, someone else tells me the new term is also wrong — and that the old one was actually preferred all along by many of the people it describes.

Welcome to disability language. It moves, it evolves, and it does not always move in one direction.

The big debate you'll encounter is person-first versus identity-first language.

Person-first language puts the person before the condition: "a person with a disability", "a person with autism", "a person who is blind." The thinking is that the person is not defined by their disability — they are a whole human being first, and the condition is secondary.

Identity-first language does the opposite: "a disabled person", "an autistic person", "a blind person." The thinking here is that the disability is not separate from the person — it is part of their identity, their experience, their culture. Many autistic people actively prefer identity-first and find person-first language patronising, as though the condition were something shameful to be kept at a polite distance.

Now here is where it gets interesting. In February 2024, Spain amended Article 49 of its constitution, removing the word "minusválido" — which translates roughly as "invalid" and had been in the constitution since 1978 — and replacing it with "personas con discapacidad": people with disability. Person-first, enshrined in law.

As someone who lives in Spain and teaches accessibility for a living, I find this genuinely complicated.

On one hand: removing "minusválido" from the constitution was long overdue, and absolutely right. On the other hand, legislating for person-first language specifically sits uncomfortably with the social model and the human rights model of disability — two frameworks that many disabled people and advocates consider foundational. The social model says that disability is not an intrinsic property of a person: it is created by the barriers society erects. Under that model, "disabled person" is not a slur. It is an accurate description of what society does to people. Many disabled people in the UK, for instance, would be actively offended by person-first language precisely because they see themselves as disabled by the world around them, not by their own bodies or minds.

Codifying one approach in law, however well-intentioned, rather closes the door on that conversation.

So which is correct? Both, and neither, universally. Different communities have different preferences. Individual people within those communities have their own. And those preferences change over time, as disabled people lead the conversation about how they want to be described.

In practice: if you know the preference of the person or community you are writing about, use it. If you don't know, person-first is generally the safer default in professional contexts — but be prepared to update if someone tells you otherwise, and do so without making a big deal of it.

The worst thing you can do is agonise so much over terminology that you stop talking about disability altogether. That helps nobody.

Hit reply if this has ever tripped you up — I'd genuinely love to know.

Piccia

## **Friday 8 May 2026 – captions**

Hey {{contact.first\_name}},

 

Do you still buy loads of courses and programs? 

I do: I am a sucker for further education. I am an eternal learner\! I seek and consume knowledge constantly and that's also why I love to teach: because every time that I have to deliver a training, I need to diligently prepare myself, and make sure my information on the topic is on point.

But beyond design and accessibility, I also gladly and humbly spend money in order to receive instructions on marketing and sales: a discipline I hope to get much better at. 

So the other week I decided to invest a considerable chunk of my education budget on a marketing and sales course that sounded right up my street. 

Today I finally carved out some time to get started with it, so, bright-eyed and bushy-tailed, eager beaver full of beans, off I go to watch the first video in the course\! 

And that's when, shock horror, I find out that the videos have closed captions, AKA burnt in so they can't be turned off. And that would be enough of a problem: but the real clincher is that each word flashes when it's uttered. 

If you've been reading my emails for a while, you've probably heard before that I have a vestibular disorder: any animation I can't control makes me feel like I'm inside a washing machine during the spin cycle.

For someone with a vestibular disorder (hello), or epilepsy (more people than you'd think), or neurodivergent, or simply deaf or hard of hearing i.e. the very category captions were created for, this type of "fancy" caption achieves the opposite effect. I literally cannot watch the video in the course I bought: the animation effect is too distracting, but more importantly it actually makes me sick, and I wish I were exaggerating\! 

 Now, of course I know that the vast majority of people who don’t have any of the ailments I described above, are armed with the best of intentions and simply can't even imagine that using that kind of captions is actually causing a problem, rather than solving it (deaf people also hate them). The makers of the course I purchased are great people who just didn't know they'd create a barrier with those captions. 

However, all of this means that the talk I gave on captions a while ago is as relevant as ever. In this day and age of fancy AI captions absolutely everywhere, this is a real issue not just for me, but for millions of other people:  

[We need to talk about captions](https://equalizedigital.com/we-need-to-talk-about-captions-piccia-neri/)  
   
You don't have to watch the whole video of course. This is what should be done instead: 

* Simply never ever use open captions with special effects, and/or any captions that can't be turned off.  
* Edit the captions if possible,  
* and activate the closed caption setting in whatever video platform you are using.  
* Also make the transcript available if you can, that's another basic accessibility requirement. 

What about you? Do you find fancy captions entertaining, or harmless – or as fun as being trapped in the spin cycle? I really want to know. 

 

In the meantime, it’s Friday\! May your weekend bring you everything you wish for.

Piccia

##  **Friday 15 May 2026 – Kintsugi**

Hey {{contact.first\_name}},

Do you know the wonderful Japanese art called Kintsugi?

A piece of porcelain breaks. But instead of cursing, picking up the pieces and throwing them away, you patiently seam it back together with gold. 

A beautiful object is transformed and gets a new lease of life, and imperfections become precious, in lieu of being seen as flaws.

It's an acceptance of fracture and breakages as part of life: finding beauty in change, impermanence, and lived experience. 

I love it: both aesthetically, and as a philosophy.

 

What if we applied this approach to every aspect of our lives? And especially to human bodies? Restoring beauty and appreciation, instead of putting up with a perceived failure.  

Old age as Kintsugi\! 

And by extension: 

Accessibility remediation as Kintsugi\! 

Threads of pure gold running through our digital assets\! 

And the added advantage of the delight of those who you are now letting in, instead of shutting them out. 

Earning their trust, instead of causing them frustration\!  

This is the thing about accessibility: I just can't bring myself to trust those who don't care about it – even when told (see my email from last week). 

I totally accept that many people simply don't know any better, and that's why I am on a mission to educate. But when they are told and still don't get it, one main thing happens. 

Trust breaks.  

What else are they going to not care about? In which other way will they trample on other people's rights? What other essential issue are they going to dismiss and minimise? It cannot end well, ever. 

And do I want to give my money to someone I don't trust? 

Nope. I don't. Neither do you, neither do disabled people who get shut out of a digital environment, and neither do all their family and friends. 

Yes, because there is one interesting thing about trust: it is not given – it is earned. And once trust is broken, it's very difficult to repair it. I'm not saying it's not possible to mend broken trust: but it does need a lot more gold than a porcelain vase. 

So, in a nutshell: 

Being inaccessible costs more money than you think, because the people who feel betrayed in their trust will want their money back from you – or may choose to never give you their money at all. 

That's my message for this week\! 

And look, it's Friday again\! I wish you the best possible weekend . 

Piccia

## **Friday 22 May 2026 – Crime doesn’t pay**

Do you also think that you must be the only one to have a certain flaw, or eccentric behaviour, or slightly shame-inducing quirk, even – but hope that you are not, because having company would make you feel less of a failure/ weirdo? 

For instance, I recently posted in a community I am part of:

"Please tell me I am not the only one that insults Claude when it fvcks up and uses all my tokens while ignoring my very clear instructions."

(I had just told Claude, verbatim: "will you take all of those em dashes and shove them up your virtual arse.")

The same thing happens when I despair because a product or website is inaccessible to me. Yes, I have a few quirks. One of them, as you might remember, is my vestibular disorder, which prevents me from enjoying crazy captions, autoplay videos, Geocities-style websites, emails full of GIFs, and the like.

My immediate reaction, when confronted with one such barrier that instantly sends my stomach on a vertiginous rollercoaster, is "I am a weirdo and a loser. Nobody else is like me. I'm the only one quietly vomiting in a corner right now."

But you know what? That's bollocks.

Here's some figures from around the world: 

35% of US adults age 40+  (ca 69 million) present evidence of vestibular dysfunction. (source: article by Yuri Agrawal, Johns Hopkins University School of Medicine, Baltimore, MD (USA))

Nope. Not just me then: in the US \*only\*, the number of people affected is the equivalent of the whole population of Thailand. Let that sink in. 

Still in the US: 

* 4% (8 million) US adults report  chronic balance problems.    
* 1.1% report chronic dizziness.    
* 80% age 65+ have experienced dizziness. 

(Source: National Institute on Deafness and other Communication Disorders (USA))

The world over, here are the numbers in other categories that are negatively affected by reckless animations: 

* 2.2 billion with a vision disability  
* 75 million on the autistic spectrum  
* 50 million with epilepsy  
* 78 million with dyslexia  
* etc

 So: I think I've made my point.

Flashing captions won't just make little me sick. They will make billions of other people sick, too. 

Just say no. 

 

 

And look my friend, lo and behold, here's Friday once more, as if by magic\!

 

Alas, I won't be able to sleep in tomorrow am because I'm giving a talk at VLC Tech Fest at crack of dawn (i.e. 9:30am, yes, that's technically dawn on a Saturday). Any Valencianes in the room? Do come say hi if so\! 

 

May your weekend bring all that you wish from it.

 

Piccia

 

 

p.s. For those of you who have been following the flashing captions course saga with bated breath: yes, of course, he did refund the course, although only after being confronted with the stark reality of the law. Bogus T\&Cs and reckless animations, like crime, don't pay\! 

##  

## **Friday 29 May 2026 – Knock me over with or without a feather**

Hey Test, 

I was speaking at VLC Tech Fest just  last Saturday, which all went very well thank you very much.

The gender ratio in the attendance was 80 men to 20 women at best,  90% white, except, notably, the talk on women in tech as an untapped resource, which ran a full inversion of that ratio, I'd say even 90 to 10\.

A ratio that shifted a little further after I went into the hallway and grabbed a few men-presenting folks by the scruff of the neck, figuratively of course, but with the same effect.

(Mate: we already know. We need YOU in the room.)

Next week I am speaking at UX London, so yesterday I finally sat down and went through the speakers line-up properly. Strange that I hadn't done it sooner as equity of representation is one of my criteria for acceptance, but anyway.

Well knock me over with a feather.

What a slap in the face of every tech conference that claims it is simply not possible to have a truly representative line-up: multiple perspectives, multiple faces, multiple experiences, rather than the same default one we've all been staring at for decades.

![][image7]

The UX London 2026 website showing all nine Discovery day speakers for Tuesday 2 June, arranged in a grid of circular portrait photos on a dark navy background. The speakers are: Maria Isachenko, UX Researcher (talk, research); Melin Edomwonyi, UX Director at Seedable Studio (talk, strategy); Marley Dizney Swanson, Product Manager at Bolt Insight (talk, research); Luisa Berta, Senior Product Designer (talk, learning); Feyikemi Akinwolemiwa, Research and Design Manager at National Grid (workshop, strategy); Natasha den Dekker, Senior UX Researcher at Made Tech (workshop, research); Chris How, Head of Experience Design at Clearleft (workshop, IA); Lucrezia Ponzano, Collaboration Strategist and Facilitator (workshop, alignment); and Michael Kibedi, Design Researcher.

Yes, my friend. It is possible. UX London has done it. Clearleft has done it. Go look for yourself: UX London 2026 speakers.

I'm speaking on Design day, Wednesday 3 June, on the intersection of creativity and accessibility. And I'm thrilled to be part of this line-up: even more so than I already was. 

If you're coming, come and find me. If you're not, maybe next year.

Oh and look, yes you guessed, it's Friday\! And on top of the delight that this always brings: it's also my birthday.

I hope it's a great day for you, too\! 

Piccia

## **Friday 5 June 2026 – UX London**

Hey

There’s one thing that will never fail to entertain me whenever I visit London: and that’s the incredibly imaginative ways in which public transport inconveniences its users. It’s so inventive that it actually ends up being quite entertaining. 

Basically, you operate embracing the full knowledge that whatever travel decision you make, it will be the wrong one. It’s kind of oddly liberating\! 

Last night, in order to get home after dinner with friends, these were my steps:

* Go to Northern line tube station at Waterloo (line was running before dinner).  
* Northern line suspended.   
* Go to Waterloo Bridge (open in both directions before dinner) to catch northbound bus.  
* Northbound lane on Waterloo Bridge closed.   
* Jump in black cab (that’s a taxi).  
* Realise that road towards Blackfriars Bridge is gridlocked.   
* Pay driver £6 for the 50 metres travelled.  
* Walk to Blackfriars Bridge, overtaking hundreds of stuck cars and buses.  
* Jump in another black cab which for £16 takes me to Moorgate in about 7 minutes (that’s more than 2 quid per km, bargain innit).  
* At Moorgate, catch northbound train.  
* Get off at wrong stop.   
* Try and fail to get an Uber.  
* Get back on next train.  
* Get off train at correct stop.   
* Try and fail to get an Uber.  
* Get a Lime bike with the last 12% left on my phone battery.   
* HOME\! 

This is all very helpful to help me cure my rose-tinted nostalgia of the Big Smoke (I lived in London for a couple of decades and love it much more now that I don’t live there, than when I did). 

But on a design-related note:

How could the travel apps I used have better helped me in my quest to return to the North London home where I’m staying?

Kind of obvious, but: it would be nice to have updated information. But I know this is dependent on lots of different variables and inputs, and it might fail. No, that’s not it. 

I finally got on the electric bike for the remaining short stretch home under duresse given that Uber was a wasteland: I’d much rather have been driven, but after my odyssey at that point I had entirely sobered up and I felt safe enough to ride. 

And both Google Maps and Citymapper insisted on showing me a route home that went through a park and along a canal, and that I know for a fact is unsafe even during the daytime. 

Travel apps really should have a “safer option” route, even if it’s longer. 

Why can’t we have this filter? Do you know? 

Curious to find out. 

P.s. UX London was utterly brilliant, and you’ll hear all about it next week.
